author: The Engineer · W-03 extension
voice_lock: Motoi Copilot dryer register — checked against 3 source samples per book (glances glance-item, scenarios :teach, scenarios :pattern).

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K' on the inner shank. What are you looking at?

Solid 14-karat gold (58.3% gold, alloy remainder).

The mark '14K' is the US standard for 14-karat gold. Solid, not plated. Confirm by weight and by wear pattern — solid gold wears uniformly; plated wears through.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mark reads as fineness declaration; assumed genuine until contradicted.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K GP' on the inner shank. What's this?

Gold-plated ring — thin gold coating over base metal.

GP = gold-plated. The metal underneath is usually brass or nickel. Wear will show through in months to years of daily wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-letter suffix qualifies the fineness — 'GP' means the metal is not solid.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '1/20 14K GF'. What's your read?

Gold-filled — mechanically bonded gold layer at 5% of the ring's weight.

Gold-filled is thicker than plated and wears far longer. Marked as fraction (1/20 = 5%) plus karat plus GF.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fraction-plus-karat-plus-GF is the gold-filled signature stamp.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped '925'. Call it.

Sterling silver — 92.5% silver, 7.5% copper.

925 is the international sterling standard, stamped as a numeric fineness mark. If the ring is also yellow-toned externally, it is likely vermeil or gold-plated sterling.

provenance.abduction_hint: Three-digit fineness mark is a purity claim expressed per-thousand.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped '.999'. What are you looking at?

Fine silver — 99.9% silver.

Fine silver is softer than sterling and tarnishes less. Often seen in coin-silver reworks or high-purity casting.

provenance.abduction_hint: Higher fineness suggests softer wear and finer craft.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, no mark visible on inside or outside. What's this?

Unknown — could be silver, stainless, nickel alloy, or pot-metal.

No mark is not evidence of no value — many older US pieces and modern craft pieces are unmarked. Weigh, test with magnet (silver is non-magnetic), and if still unknown use a silver-testing solution or XRF.

provenance.abduction_hint: Absence of mark shifts burden to physical tests — density, magnetism, chemistry.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'PLAT'. What's your read?

Platinum — one of the densest jewelry metals, unusually valuable.

Platinum marks: PLAT, PT950, PT900, 950, 900. Very heavy for size. Cool to the touch. Does not tarnish. Ten times rarer in jewelry than gold.

provenance.abduction_hint: Four-letter root PLAT is unambiguous — always investigate further.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '18K 750'. Call it.

Solid 18-karat gold (75% gold), marked both US and European conventions.

18K = 750 (per thousand). Dual-marking is common on pieces made for export or European-market resale. Softer than 14K, warmer yellow.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two marks that agree on the same fineness reinforce the claim.

messages.content: A ring, rose-toned, stamped '14K'. What are you looking at?

Rose gold — 14K gold alloyed with a higher proportion of copper.

Rose gold's pink comes from copper. The mark stays 14K because it is still 58.3% gold; only the alloy composition of the remainder changes.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color shift with same fineness stamp implies alloy composition change.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped '585' with a small crown mark next to it. What's this?

14-karat white gold, European hallmark (crown indicates gold assay).

585 = 14K (58.5% per thousand — Europe rounds; US calls it 14K = 58.3%). White gold is nickel- or palladium-alloyed and often rhodium-plated for the bright white finish.

provenance.abduction_hint: Numeric fineness plus symbol signals European assay-office hallmark.

messages.content: A necklace clasp with '925 ITALY' and a small oval maker mark. What's your read?

Sterling silver, Italian-made, with a registered maker mark.

Italian sterling is a major supply category. The maker mark is usually a registered stamp — worth photographing and looking up if you resell.

provenance.abduction_hint: Country-of-origin mark plus maker mark on clasp tells provenance quickly.

messages.content: A bracelet stamped '10K' inside the clasp. Call it.

Solid 10-karat gold — the lowest karat legally called 'gold' in the US.

10K is 41.7% gold. Harder and more durable than higher karats. Common for daily-wear pieces and men's rings. Value calculation uses the 0.417 factor against gold spot price.

provenance.abduction_hint: Low karat number still qualifies as gold — the mark is the license.

messages.content: A pendant stamped '22K'. What are you looking at?

Solid 22-karat gold — 91.7% gold, common in South Asian and Middle Eastern jewelry.

22K is very soft — hallmarks may look shallow because the metal deforms easily. Preferred in cultures where jewelry doubles as savings; often unmarked in modern Indian gold but stamped '22K' for export.

provenance.abduction_hint: High karat plus soft edges implies regional jewelry tradition.

messages.content: A ring stamped '24K' on the outside band. What's this?

Suspicious — 24K is very soft and rarely used for durable jewelry.

Real 24K exists but is unusual as a ring. External stamping (rather than inside the band) is a fake-marker red flag. Test before believing.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stamp placement outside the band is atypical — worth suspicion.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped 'HGE'. What's your read?

Heavy Gold Electroplate — thick plating, but plating nonetheless.

HGE indicates plating heavier than standard GP but still not solid or gold-filled. Older fashion-jewelry mark. Value is largely the design, not the metal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Three-letter suffix that includes 'E' or 'P' usually indicates a plating type.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'STER' on the inner band. Call it.

Sterling silver — 'STER' is the abbreviated older US mark for sterling.

US pieces from the mid-20th century often used 'STER' or 'STERLING' spelled out rather than '925'. Both refer to the same alloy.

provenance.abduction_hint: Word-form marks are American mid-century; numeric fineness marks are international and modern.

messages.content: A cuff stamped 'MEXICO 925' with a stylized artist mark. What are you looking at?

Mexican sterling silver, artist-signed.

Mexican silver has a substantial studio tradition — Taxco pieces from mid-century onward are collector-grade. Look up the artist mark; some marks multiply value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Country origin plus artist mark plus fineness clusters as artisan provenance.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, with no mark and a magnet sticks to it. What's this?

Not gold — likely gold-plated steel or gold-tone base metal.

Gold is not magnetic. Neither is silver, copper, brass, or platinum. If a magnet sticks, the core is a magnetic metal — usually steel or nickel.

provenance.abduction_hint: Magnet test is a fast disqualifier — rules out precious metal cores.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, with no mark and a magnet does not stick. What's your read?

Ambiguous — could be silver, aluminum, pot-metal, or nickel-based alloy. Further testing required.

Non-magnetic rules out steel and iron but leaves many options. Weight, sound (silver rings musically when tapped), and chemical test resolve.

provenance.abduction_hint: Negative magnet result is necessary but not sufficient for precious metal.

messages.content: A pendant stamped '925' with the letter 'S' inside a shield next to the mark. Call it.

Sterling silver with a maker's shield touchmark — worth researching the shield symbol.

Shield-enclosed maker marks are common European and British tradition. The exact shield shape and the letter identify the maker; databases and hallmark books exist for lookup.

provenance.abduction_hint: Enclosed-letter mark is a maker signature — investigate for value bump.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, with '925' and 'CZ' both stamped inside the band. What are you looking at?

Sterling silver setting with cubic-zirconia stone.

CZ stamped near the setting is the ethical disclosure that the clear stone is cubic zirconia, not diamond. Common on affordable engagement-style pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two adjacent marks describe metal and stone simultaneously.

messages.content: A chain, yellow-toned, with '14K' stamped on the clasp. What's this?

Solid 14-karat gold chain.

Chain marks are stamped on the clasp or on a small metal tag near the clasp. On very fine chains the mark may be tiny and require magnification.

provenance.abduction_hint: Chain marks live at the clasp — habitually check there first.

messages.content: A chain, yellow-toned, with '14K GF' stamped on the clasp. What's your read?

Gold-filled chain — a durable middle tier between plated and solid.

Gold-filled chains hold up to daily wear for years, unlike plated. Preferred by makers who want a solid-gold look at a lower price point.

provenance.abduction_hint: GF suffix on chain = mechanical bond, expect long life.

messages.content: A pearl necklace, uniform white pearls, tap on your teeth feels slippery. Call it.

Imitation — likely glass or plastic. Real pearls feel gritty on the tooth.

The 'tooth test' — genuine pearl nacre has microscopic texture; a real pearl feels gritty. Imitation pearls feel smooth. Combine with visual: real pearls have tiny surface variations.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniformity plus tactile smoothness converge on imitation.

messages.content: A pearl necklace, pearls slightly irregular, gritty on the tooth. What are you looking at?

Real pearls — likely freshwater or cultured.

Tooth-test grit plus surface irregularity is the classic real-pearl combination. Freshwater pearls are common, affordable, and often irregular. Saltwater pearls (akoya, south sea, tahitian) are usually rounder and more uniform but still gritty.

provenance.abduction_hint: Irregularity plus grit = organic origin, likely real pearl.

messages.content: A bracelet with what look like diamonds all around, no stamp on the metal. What's this?

Unknown — could be genuine tennis bracelet, could be CZ or crystal on plated base. Test the metal first.

A missing stamp is the loudest signal here. A genuine diamond tennis bracelet would ordinarily carry a metal mark (14K or 18K or PLAT). No mark means either an antique from before mandatory marking or a costume piece.

provenance.abduction_hint: Absence of stamp on a piece pretending to value is the strongest single skeptical signal.

messages.content: A pair of earrings stamped '925' on the posts. What's your read?

Sterling silver posts — often the whole earring is sterling but sometimes only the posts.

Sterling posts are hypoallergenic-adjacent (nickel-free unless argentium is not used) and reduce ear irritation. When only the post is sterling and the front is plated brass, some sellers still stamp only the post — read the listing carefully.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mark on posts alone is common; mark on the ornamental portion is more informative.

messages.content: A pair of earrings with a small stamp reading '585' and a triangle. Call it.

14K gold, European hallmark (triangle indicates gold assay in some countries).

European hallmarks combine numeric fineness with a symbol indicating metal type — triangle, crown, or head for gold; various for silver. Not every country uses the same symbol.

provenance.abduction_hint: Numeric plus symbol combination is diagnostic of European origin.

messages.content: A ring band with '925' and a small stamp of a fish. What are you looking at?

Sterling silver with a Danish or Scandinavian maker mark (fish symbols are used by several Nordic maker traditions).

Symbol marks after the fineness often identify country or maker. Scandinavian silver has a strong studio tradition; some fish-marks are highly desirable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Symbol adjacent to fineness invites lookup for provenance premium.

messages.content: A signet ring, gold-toned, engraved with initials, no fineness mark. What's this?

Personal signet — the engraved initials reduce resale unless the ring is historically or maker-notable. Test the metal.

Signet rings without marks may be pre-1900 pieces (predating standard hallmarking laws in many jurisdictions), family heirlooms, or costume. Testing solves the metal question; provenance research determines whether initials add or subtract value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Personalization is context-dependent: adds value when historically important, subtracts when generic.

messages.content: A bracelet, yellow-toned, marked '1/10 12K GF'. What's your read?

Gold-filled — 10% of weight is 12K gold, mechanically bonded.

Higher-fraction gold-filled (1/10 vs 1/20) means proportionally more gold and better wear. 12K is a slightly lower karat than 14K but still substantial.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bigger numerator in the fraction = more gold — worth more.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'RGP'. Call it.

Rolled Gold Plate — older term for gold-filled, common on Victorian and Edwardian pieces.

RGP predates the modern 'GF' marking convention. Frequently seen on antique watch chains and lockets. Wears well due to mechanical bond.

provenance.abduction_hint: Three-letter marks starting with R often indicate rolled or reinforced construction.

messages.content: A necklace stamped 'VERMEIL' on the clasp. What are you looking at?

Vermeil — sterling silver base with at least 10 microinches of gold plating.

Vermeil is the US legal term for 'gold-over-sterling' with a minimum plating thickness. Wears better than gold-plated brass. Priced above plated, below gold-filled.

provenance.abduction_hint: Explicit VERMEIL word-stamp is a legal-compliance mark.

messages.content: A necklace stamped 'RHODIUM PLATED' on the clasp. What's this?

The finish is rhodium plating — usually over sterling or over white gold. The underlying metal is not disclosed by this mark alone.

Rhodium is a thin, bright, protective plating. Common over white gold to give the brilliant-white appearance. When the rhodium wears, the underlying metal (which may be slightly yellow-tinged white gold) shows.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plating disclosure without base-metal disclosure requires further investigation.

messages.content: A ring stamped '18K' outside on the band, in raised letters. What's your read?

Suspicious — raised (cast-in) marks are less trustworthy than stamped (post-cast) marks.

Genuine hallmarks are almost always struck AFTER casting or fabrication, leaving a slightly recessed impression. Raised marks that are part of the mold are common on costume pieces and counterfeits.

provenance.abduction_hint: Direction of the mark impression (recessed vs raised) is a genuineness signal.

messages.content: A silver-toned ring, no mark, band shows brass-yellow when scratched inside. Call it.

Silver-plated brass — the base metal is exposed by the scratch.

The scratch test on the least-visible part of a piece is a common trade test. Silver over brass is the most common costume construction. Value is design-only.

provenance.abduction_hint: Base-metal color under a scratch is a definitive metal identifier.

messages.content: A yellow-toned ring, no mark, band shows silver-white when scratched inside. What are you looking at?

Gold-plated silver, or gold-plated white base metal — possibly vermeil if the silver is sterling.

Silver-white under yellow plating narrows to two possibilities: gold-plated sterling (vermeil) or gold-plated nickel/steel. A magnet test resolves — magnet stick means nickel/steel base.

provenance.abduction_hint: Chain scratch test → magnet test → precious-metal test narrows metal identity in three steps.

messages.content: A ring stamped '925 THAI'. What's this?

Sterling silver, Thai-made.

Thai silver is a major supply source — Karen hill-tribe silver and Bangkok manufacturing both stamp origin. Karen hill-tribe silver is often higher-fineness (95-99%) than sterling.

provenance.abduction_hint: Origin marks after fineness identify supply-chain source.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, with '10K' and a small maker's initial in an oval. What's your read?

Solid 10K gold with a US maker's mark.

US makers self-stamp — there is no legal US hallmark system. Common practice is a small oval or lozenge with initials or a logo, next to the karat mark. Some marks are worth researching.

provenance.abduction_hint: Oval maker mark adjacent to karat is standard US construction.

messages.content: A cuff, silver-toned, stamped 'ALPACA'. Call it.

German silver / nickel silver — not silver at all. A copper-nickel-zinc alloy.

Alpaca (also 'alpacca') is the trade name in some regions for nickel silver. Contains no silver. Common in Mexican and South American craft jewelry. Costume-tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: 'Alpaca' is a false-friend word — sounds like silver, is not.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'SILVER' but no fineness number. What are you looking at?

Suspicious — genuine silver almost always carries a fineness mark. 'SILVER' alone may be a costume-tier or generic stamp.

The word 'SILVER' without a fineness number is not a legally-defined claim. Could be silver, could be silver-tone. Test to verify.

provenance.abduction_hint: Word-only fineness claim without number is under-specified — treat skeptically.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'COIN'. What's this?

Coin silver — usually 90% silver, 10% copper. Older US standard, pre-sterling.

Coin silver was the US standard before sterling; pieces marked COIN are often mid-1800s. Slightly less pure than sterling. Collector-grade in some contexts.

provenance.abduction_hint: Old US mark categories predate modern sterling standardization.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '9K'. What's your read?

Solid 9-karat gold — the British minimum-legal-gold standard (37.5% gold).

9K is legal to call 'gold' in the UK and Commonwealth but not in the US (US minimum is 10K). Common on British and Australian jewelry. Slightly less warm-yellow than higher karats due to more copper.

provenance.abduction_hint: Karat below 10 signals non-US-market origin.

messages.content: A brooch, yellow-toned, stamped '15K'. Call it.

Older gold standard — 15K was legal in Britain from 1854 to 1932. Antique piece.

15K marks predate 1932 in Britain; often paired with a Chester, Birmingham, or London assay mark and a date letter. Excellent age-marker.

provenance.abduction_hint: Non-standard modern karats often signal antique origin — dating opportunity.

messages.content: A cuff, silver-toned, stamped 'FINE SILVER'. What are you looking at?

Fine silver — 99.9% silver, softer than sterling, less tarnish.

Word-form marks for high-purity silver are common on studio pieces. Fine silver deforms more easily than sterling — think about durability for daily wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Word-form claim of 'fine' silver combined with soft feel confirms high purity.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '18K' and '750' with a small 'i' inside a hexagon. What's this?

18K gold, Italian hallmark. The hexagonal mark indicates Italy.

Italy uses a hexagonal maker's mark containing a number (identifying the province and manufacturer) — a full lookup requires the province code. Adds provenance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hexagonal enclosure of a symbol is Italy-specific hallmark convention.

messages.content: A pendant, silver-toned, stamped '830'. What's your read?

Continental European silver — 83% pure. Common in Scandinavian, German, and Baltic pieces.

830 silver was the German standard until 1888, and continued in Scandinavia. Slightly softer than sterling, distinct provenance. Marker for older continental European work.

provenance.abduction_hint: Non-standard silver finenesses (800, 830, 875, 900) signal continental European tradition.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped '800'. Call it.

Silver at 80% purity — common in older German, Italian, and Middle Eastern pieces.

800 silver was widely used across continental Europe pre-1900. Not sterling by modern standards but legally silver in many jurisdictions. Look at style for approximate date.

provenance.abduction_hint: Low silver fineness = older piece or continental origin.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, stamped '14K PLUMB' or '14KP'. What are you looking at?

Plumb 14K — exactly 14K (58.3%), no legal tolerance below.

In the US, standard 14K allows a small tolerance below 14K. 'Plumb' (P) certifies the metal is exactly 14K or higher. Signals precision maker.

provenance.abduction_hint: P suffix on karat is a precision-tolerance guarantee.

messages.content: A cuff, yellow-toned, stamped '14KT' (with T). What's this?

14-karat gold — the T stands for 'karat' in older US convention. Solid gold.

Older US pieces often stamp 14KT or 18KT. Same meaning as modern 14K or 18K. Age-marker for pieces from mid-20th century and earlier.

provenance.abduction_hint: T suffix is an archaic spelling convention — same meaning.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'PLAT 900 IRID 100'. What's your read?

Platinum-iridium alloy — 90% platinum, 10% iridium. Traditional US platinum-jewelry alloy.

Iridium hardens platinum for setting durability. This alloy was standard for US platinum jewelry from early 1900s through mid-century. Very durable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-component precious-metal mark = named alloy composition.

messages.content: A ring stamped '925 STERLING BALI'. Call it.

Sterling silver, Bali-style construction — Indonesian handmade tradition, granulation and wirework common.

Bali silver is a distinct visual style — heavy granulation, twisted wire, tribal motifs. Handmade. Some makers signed, most unsigned. Look at the workmanship for value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Origin word plus style word co-locates handmade tradition.

messages.content: A necklace stamped '925 CHINA'. What are you looking at?

Sterling silver, Chinese-made — often mass-produced, sometimes studio.

Chinese sterling is a huge supply category with quality that varies from mass-market to high studio. The mark tells you sterling and Chinese origin; the style and workmanship tell you tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Origin mark alone does not determine tier — inspect workmanship.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'TITANIUM'. What's this?

Titanium — not silver, not gold, but a modern hypoallergenic metal.

Titanium jewelry is lightweight, hypoallergenic, and cannot be sized (does not solder like precious metals). Modern-only. Priced by design, not by metal weight.

provenance.abduction_hint: Metal-name word-mark disclaims precious-metal claim entirely.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'TUNGSTEN' or 'TUNGSTEN CARBIDE'. What's your read?

Tungsten carbide — extremely hard modern metal, cannot be sized or resoldered.

Tungsten rings are popular for men's wedding bands due to durability. Cannot be cut off by ordinary means (requires specialized crackers). Priced by design, not by metal weight.

provenance.abduction_hint: Modern-metal word-marks all indicate industrial-alloy construction.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'STAINLESS' or '316L'. Call it.

Stainless steel — usually surgical-grade (316L) for hypoallergenic wear.

316L is the medical-grade stainless standard. Common on body jewelry, sports jewelry, and inexpensive fashion pieces. Non-magnetic in typical alloys but some cheaper stainless is weakly magnetic.

provenance.abduction_hint: Number-plus-letter (316L) is a stainless-steel grade signature.

messages.content: A pendant stamped '.925' with a hyphen — '.925-'. What are you looking at?

Sterling silver — the punctuation is common on some maker stamps and doesn't change the meaning.

Some stamps include punctuation (.925 or .925-) as part of the die design. Meaning is unchanged: sterling silver. Focus on the number.

provenance.abduction_hint: Punctuation on marks is stylistic — read past it to the number.

messages.content: A ring, no mark, tap-tested rings musically for a long time. What's this?

Consistent with silver — silver rings musically when tapped. Not conclusive alone.

The tap test: hold the ring loosely, tap gently against a hard surface. Silver rings clearly and holds the note. Base metals thud or ring briefly. Combine with weight, color, and chemical test.

provenance.abduction_hint: Acoustic test supplements visual and tactile — sums to identification.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, gives a slight green streak on a ceramic streak-plate. What's your read?

Not gold — gold does not streak on ceramic. Likely brass or gold-plated brass.

The streak test: gold is too dense and non-friable to leave a streak on unglazed ceramic. Brass leaves a slight yellow-green streak. Rough test — modern acid-testing is more definitive.

provenance.abduction_hint: Streak test is a rough sorter — negative for gold, positive for brass.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' with a rich buttery shine and no reddish tone. Call it.

Solid 14K gold, most likely yellow gold (as opposed to rose or green).

Color of solid gold shifts with alloy: rose (copper-rich), green (silver-rich), yellow (balanced). Rich buttery yellow is the typical US 14K appearance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color-family read plus mark confirms alloy variant.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' with a faint reddish tone. What are you looking at?

14K rose gold or a copper-heavy alloy — still 58.3% gold, but alloy composition tilts pink.

Rose tone from a 14K piece indicates the alloy metals are copper-heavier than balanced yellow. Same fineness, different visual class.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color of the alloy is independent of the fineness of gold.

messages.content: A ring, yellow-toned, marked '14K,' looking pale and almost greenish. What's this?

14K green gold — alloy is silver-heavy. Less common.

Green-gold alloy uses silver and cadmium (or silver alone) instead of copper. Same 58.3% gold. Visually distinct — the pale green tone is subtle and easy to miss.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pale-green tone in gold signals unusual alloy — investigate further.

messages.content: A ring, white-metal, stamped '14K,' with a slight yellow underhue. What's your read?

White gold with rhodium plating that has worn — the underlying alloy is white-ish but not bright white.

New white gold is often rhodium-plated for brilliant whiteness. As rhodium wears, the underlying alloy shows through, typically slightly warmer. Re-plating restores the bright finish.

provenance.abduction_hint: Yellowing under white surface = rhodium wear-through, not composition change.

messages.content: A ring, white-metal, marked '14K,' brilliant bright white. Call it.

White gold, likely freshly rhodium-plated. Could also be palladium white gold (naturally whiter, less need for rhodium).

Palladium white gold is a modern alternative to nickel white gold — brighter naturally, less rhodium dependence, hypoallergenic. Mark alone does not distinguish.

provenance.abduction_hint: Brilliant whiteness signals plating or premium alloy — needs metal-test to confirm.

messages.content: A ring stamped '14K' and 'C.T.'. What are you looking at?

Solid 14K gold; the C.T. is likely a maker's initials or an old jewelry retailer signature.

Initial-marks after karat are typically maker or retailer signatures. Some retailer marks (Tiffany, Cartier — do NOT invoke by name in operator listings) can multiply value. Research any unfamiliar initials.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-initial mark is a hallmark or brand — investigate.

messages.content: A ring with a large clear stone that fogs briefly when breathed on. What's this?

Suspicious for diamond — genuine diamonds disperse fog almost instantly due to high thermal conductivity.

The fog test: exhale on a stone. Diamond clears in under a second. Moissanite clears in 1-2 seconds. Cubic zirconia holds the fog for several seconds. Not definitive alone — a thermal tester is more reliable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Thermal-conductivity behavior on fog test separates stone types.

messages.content: A ring with a large clear stone that shows rainbow flashes at every angle. What's your read?

Likely moissanite or high-quality CZ — the color dispersion (fire) is higher than diamond's.

Moissanite has 2.4× the dispersion of diamond, giving more visible rainbow-flash. Trained buyers can spot moissanite by the excess-of-fire signature. CZ also over-disperses.

provenance.abduction_hint: Excess fire is diagnostic of higher-dispersion stones than diamond.

messages.content: A ring with a large clear stone with visible internal 'garden' inclusions. Call it.

Likely emerald (jardin is normal) or a natural-inclusion diamond. Rule out based on color.

Emerald almost always shows jardin (natural inclusions) — a flawless emerald is very unusual. Included diamonds are lower-clarity grades (SI, I). The visible pattern helps identify.

provenance.abduction_hint: Inclusion patterns differ by stone — jardin is signature of emerald.

messages.content: A ring with a large clear stone that scratches under a steel-nail test. What are you looking at?

Not diamond, corundum, or moissanite — likely glass, quartz, or paste.

Anything above Mohs 5.5 will not scratch under a steel nail (steel is ~5.5). Diamond (10), corundum (9), topaz (8), quartz (7), and moissanite (9.25) are safe from a nail. Glass and softer stones scratch.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hardness test rules out durable-gem candidates.

messages.content: A ring with a green stone that shows subtle color-zoning under a loupe. What's this?

Consistent with natural emerald or natural tourmaline — synthetic stones usually lack the color-zoning.

Natural stones grow slowly in inhomogeneous conditions; color-zoning (bands or patches of different intensity) is a natural signature. Lab-grown stones often lack it or have unnaturally uniform zoning.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color-zoning is a natural-origin indicator when subtle.

messages.content: A ring with a green stone marked 'EMERALD' on the certificate — center stone shows unnatural saturated uniformity. What's your read?

Possibly lab-grown emerald (hydrothermal or flux). Certificate should disclose.

Lab-grown emeralds are chemically identical to natural but often have distinctively uniform color and different inclusion patterns (spike-like, curved striae). FTC requires disclosure — check the paperwork.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform saturation is atypical for natural emerald — investigate synthetic possibility.

messages.content: A ring with a blue stone that shows silky sheen from within. Call it.

Consistent with natural sapphire — silk inclusions (fine rutile needles) are a natural signature.

Silk in sapphire comes from tiny needle-like rutile inclusions. In heavily heat-treated sapphires the silk melts. Natural, un-heated, silked sapphires are rare and command a premium — always mention if you spot silk.

provenance.abduction_hint: Silk in blue corundum = natural, likely un-heated, higher value.

messages.content: A ring with a red stone showing strong glow under UV / longwave black-light. What are you looking at?

Consistent with natural ruby — most natural rubies fluoresce red under UV due to chromium content.

The UV fluorescence test: natural rubies (especially Burmese) glow red under longwave UV. Some synthetic rubies also fluoresce; garnet does not. Combined with other tests, useful.

provenance.abduction_hint: UV fluorescence + red color + Mohs 9 hardness converges on ruby.

messages.content: A ring with a black stone that has iridescent play-of-color. What's this?

Black opal — high-value variety of opal with dark body-color and rainbow play.

Black opal is one of the most valuable opal types; the dark body enhances the visibility of the play-of-color. Check for doublet or triplet construction (opal slice on backing) which is common and should be disclosed.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dark body plus play-of-color = black opal, top-tier variety.

messages.content: A ring with a stone showing straight lines of play-of-color across the top only. What's your read?

Likely opal doublet or triplet — the play is a thin slice on dark backing.

Doublets have a thin opal top on an onyx or glass base. Triplets add a clear cap. Side-view under magnification shows the layer boundary. Priced significantly below solid opals; disclose.

provenance.abduction_hint: Play-of-color confined to a plane = layered construction.

messages.content: A ring with a large purple stone showing color-change between daylight and incandescent light. Call it.

Likely alexandrite (chrysoberyl variety) or color-change garnet.

Alexandrite is famously bluish-green in daylight, purplish-red in incandescent. Natural alexandrite is extremely valuable. Lab-grown and color-change garnet are more common. Certificate needed to distinguish.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color-change under lighting shift = alexandrite family or color-change garnet.

messages.content: A ring with a large brown stone that shows a cat's-eye of light across it. What are you looking at?

Cat's-eye chrysoberyl or similar chatoyant stone (tigereye, cat's-eye tourmaline).

Chatoyancy is a single band of reflected light that moves with the stone. Chrysoberyl cat's-eye is the most prized. Cabochon cut is required to display the effect.

provenance.abduction_hint: Single moving light band = chatoyancy — cabochon-cut cat's-eye family.

messages.content: A ring with a stone showing a six-rayed star of light when rotated. What's this?

Star sapphire, star ruby, or star rose-quartz — asterism from oriented rutile inclusions.

Star stones require cabochon cut and specific inclusion orientation. Star sapphires and rubies are premium. Star rose-quartz is common and inexpensive. All show a six-rayed asterism.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-ray star = asterism, requires cabochon cut plus oriented inclusions.

messages.content: A ring with a large opaque green stone with black veining. What's your read?

Likely malachite or similar copper-mineral stone (chrysocolla, azurmalachite).

Malachite is a copper-carbonate with characteristic banded green pattern. Soft (Mohs 3.5-4) so it wears; use in pendants and pins more than daily-wear rings. Very distinctive appearance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Banded green with black lines = malachite family, soft stone.

messages.content: A ring with a large opaque blue stone with dark matrix veins. Call it.

Likely turquoise, potentially high-grade if the matrix pattern is spiderweb.

Turquoise ranges from cheap dyed magnesite to premium Sleeping Beauty (no matrix) or Number Eight (spiderweb matrix). Matrix pattern, color saturation, and country of origin drive value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Opaque blue with matrix = turquoise family; matrix pattern = value tier signal.

messages.content: A necklace where the chain is smooth-tubed with no visible links. What are you looking at?

Snake chain — smooth, articulated, delicate.

Snake chain construction — many small linked segments giving the appearance of a continuous tube. Elegant, but prone to kinking if bent sharply. Common at multiple metal tiers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Continuous-tube appearance with articulation = snake chain.

messages.content: A necklace where the chain is flat, woven-looking, and drapes like ribbon. What's this?

Herringbone chain.

Herringbone has a flat woven appearance, drapes beautifully, and kinks easily if handled roughly. Common in gold and sterling. Disclose the kink tendency to buyers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Flat woven ribbon-drape = herringbone construction.

messages.content: A necklace where the chain has alternating small oval and long oval links. What's your read?

Figaro chain.

Figaro chain has a repeating pattern of two or three short flat links followed by one long flat link. Italian tradition, very common at all price tiers, unisex.

provenance.abduction_hint: Alternating link sizes with clear pattern = figaro.

messages.content: A necklace where the chain looks like twisted rope. Call it.

Rope chain.

Rope chain has a twisted appearance, sometimes hollow (lighter, cheaper) sometimes solid (heavier, better wear). Popular for pendants due to good sturdiness.

provenance.abduction_hint: Twisted-braided appearance = rope construction; hollow vs solid drives price.

messages.content: A necklace with square links, minimalist appearance. What are you looking at?

Box chain.

Box chain has square cross-section links, giving a clean modern appearance. Popular for men's pendants and minimal contemporary jewelry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Square-cross-section repeated links = box chain.

messages.content: A necklace with round links all uniform size. What's this?

Cable chain — the all-purpose default construction.

Cable chain is the most common construction. Uniform round oval links. Works at any price tier. Sold by weight and length.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform ovals = cable chain, the everyday standard.

messages.content: A necklace with slightly flattened interlocking links. What's your read?

Curb chain.

Curb chain has interlocking links flattened for lie-flat wear. Common on men's chains, ID bracelets, and utility jewelry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Flattened interlocking oval links = curb.

messages.content: A necklace that looks like a chain of tiny woven baskets. Call it.

Wheat chain — braided-appearance four-strand construction.

Wheat chain has four strands braided together into a rope-like but flatter appearance. Sturdy, holds pendants well.

provenance.abduction_hint: Woven-basket repetition = wheat construction.

messages.content: A necklace with faceted, sparkling twisted links. What are you looking at?

Singapore chain — faceted for maximum light-play.

Singapore chain is a twisted chain with faceted links to catch light. Delicate, feminine, common in delicate gold and sterling.

provenance.abduction_hint: Faceted twisted-link that sparkles = Singapore construction.

messages.content: A necklace with round links joined by a small bar between each two links. What's this?

Bar-and-link chain — a stylistic variant used in modern minimal design.

Contemporary chain style. The bar-link element creates rhythm and visual interest. Priced by weight and design work.

provenance.abduction_hint: Rhythmic bar-plus-link = intentional contemporary style.

messages.content: A ring with four small metal claws holding the center stone. What's your read?

Prong setting — the classic diamond-solitaire mount.

Four-prong is the classic solitaire setting; six-prong (Tiffany-style — do not name-brand in listings) is another common variant. Prongs need periodic re-tipping as they wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Claws holding stone from above = prong setting.

messages.content: A ring with a metal collar completely wrapping the girdle of the stone. Call it.

Bezel setting — most protective mount.

Bezel setting wraps the stone's girdle in metal, protecting it. Best for active-wear pieces. Reduces light entry to the stone; may make the stone look darker than in a prong setting.

provenance.abduction_hint: Continuous metal collar around stone = bezel.

messages.content: A ring with stones set in a groove between two rails of metal. What are you looking at?

Channel setting.

Channel setting has stones secured in a groove with metal walls on both sides. Popular for eternity and wedding bands. Very durable — stones cannot easily be knocked loose.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stones flush in a metal groove = channel.

messages.content: A ring surface covered with small stones set close together like a paved road. What's this?

Pavé setting.

Pavé setting uses tiny prongs to hold small stones very close together. Rich appearance, high sparkle. Small stones can be lost if not periodically checked.

provenance.abduction_hint: Paved-road appearance of small stones = pavé.

messages.content: A ring where the center stone appears suspended between two walls of metal with no visible prongs. What's your read?

Tension setting.

Tension setting relies on metal spring pressure across the girdle. Extremely modern-looking. Requires precise workmanship and hard-metal alloys; not for soft metals.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stone suspended without visible prong or bezel = tension setting.

messages.content: A ring with a center stone encircled by a ring of small accent stones. Call it.

Halo setting.

Halo setting places small accent stones around the center stone, making it appear larger and adding sparkle. Very popular for engagement rings 2010-onward. Center stones can range from small to substantial.

provenance.abduction_hint: Center-plus-ring-of-accents = halo.

messages.content: A ring with three stones — a large center and two smaller flanking stones. What are you looking at?

Three-stone setting — often symbolic (past, present, future) in engagement contexts.

Three-stone rings can be classic (three diamonds), sentimental (birthstones of family members), or engagement (past-present-future symbolism). Setting details determine value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Center-plus-two-flankers = three-stone arrangement.

messages.content: A ring with a large center stone and a shank set with small stones. What's this?

Solitaire-with-pavé-band or accent-shank ring.

The center is a solitaire; the shank carries additional small stones. Increases sparkle and perceived weight of the piece. Common at all price tiers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Solitaire center plus decorated shank = accent-band solitaire.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond that shows a chunky, high-crown facet pattern with a small table. What's your read?

Old-mine-cut diamond — hand-cut, pre-1900, cushion-shaped.

Old-mine-cut diamonds are pre-1900 hand-cut stones with high crowns, small tables, and chunky facets. Prized by collectors for their warm, romantic light-return. Distinct from modern brilliant.

provenance.abduction_hint: High crown plus small table plus chunky facets on square-ish stone = old-mine-cut.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond that shows a flat bottom and a faceted dome. Call it.

Rose-cut diamond — historic cut, popular Victorian-era and revived contemporary.

Rose-cut diamonds have a flat bottom (no pavilion) and a faceted dome. Softer sparkle than brilliants. Historical (medieval-through-Victorian) and revived in contemporary artisan jewelry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Flat-bottom faceted-dome = rose-cut.

messages.content: A ring with a rectangular diamond, parallel step-cut facets, chamfered corners. What are you looking at?

Emerald-cut diamond — step-cut rectangular with cut corners.

Emerald-cut diamonds emphasize clarity over sparkle (parallel facets act as clarity windows). Because inclusions show, only higher-clarity stones are commercially cut this way.

provenance.abduction_hint: Rectangular with step-facets and chamfered corners = emerald-cut.

messages.content: A ring with a square diamond, four-sided step-cut. What's this?

Asscher cut — square emerald-cut variant.

Asscher cut is a square version of the emerald cut with slightly steeper steps. Vintage-modern look. Requires high-clarity stones for the same reasons emerald cut does.

provenance.abduction_hint: Square with step-cut sides = Asscher.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond having 58 facets in the classic pattern. What's your read?

Round brilliant cut — the modern standard.

The 58-facet round brilliant is the most common diamond cut, engineered for maximum light return. Standardized by Marcel Tolkowsky in 1919 and refined since.

provenance.abduction_hint: Round with 58 facets in brilliant pattern = round brilliant.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond in an elongated oval brilliant pattern. Call it.

Oval brilliant cut.

Oval brilliant is a modified round brilliant stretched into an oval. Popular for solitaires as it appears larger than a round of equal carat. Watch for the 'bowtie' effect — a dark central band that indicates poor cut.

provenance.abduction_hint: Elongated round-brilliant pattern = oval brilliant.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond in a teardrop shape. What are you looking at?

Pear-shaped brilliant cut.

Pear shape (teardrop) is a modified round with one pointed end. Requires careful setting to protect the tip. Popular for pendants and drop earrings as well as rings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Teardrop with brilliant faceting = pear shape.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond in a marquise shape — pointed at both ends. What's this?

Marquise brilliant cut.

Marquise cut is elongated with points at both ends. Named for the Marchioness of Pompadour. Points are the most vulnerable feature — often V-prong-protected.

provenance.abduction_hint: Elongated pointed-both-ends = marquise.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond in a heart shape. What's your read?

Heart brilliant cut.

Heart-cut is a modified pear or marquise cut with a cleft at one end. Requires excellent symmetry to be well-executed. Common in sentiment-jewelry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Heart outline with brilliant faceting = heart cut.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond in a rectangular cushion shape with brilliant faceting. Call it.

Cushion brilliant — modern version, sometimes called modified cushion.

Modern cushion cut is a squared-off rectangle with rounded corners and brilliant-style faceting. Distinct from the older cushion (which was step-cut or old-mine). Very popular 2010-onward.

provenance.abduction_hint: Square-with-rounded-corners brilliant = modern cushion.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond that looks like a rectangular emerald-cut but only 5-10 facets total, no crown, low profile. What are you looking at?

Baguette cut — small rectangular step-cut, usually as accent stones.

Baguettes are small rectangular step-cuts, almost never used as center stones. Common in Art Deco and modern eternity bands as accents.

provenance.abduction_hint: Small rectangular step-cut with minimal faceting = baguette accent.

messages.content: A ring with a diamond that looks like a baguette with tapered ends. What's this?

Tapered baguette.

Tapered baguettes narrow toward the ends. Often used flanking a solitaire in traditional three-stone rings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Trapezoidal accent stone with step-cut = tapered baguette.

messages.content: A necklace pendant is a smooth-domed cabochon on a yellow-gold bezel. What's your read?

Cabochon-set pendant — the stone is un-faceted, held by a metal collar.

Cabochon cut is used for opaque stones, chatoyant stones, star stones, and for style choices with translucent stones. Bezel setting protects the cabochon.

provenance.abduction_hint: Smooth-dome plus bezel = cabochon pendant construction.

messages.content: A pendant with a stone showing a play of iridescent color across its dome. Call it.

Cabochon opal — likely white or crystal opal if the body is light, black opal if dark.

Cabochon-cut opal shows play-of-color best. Body color, brightness of play, and pattern (pinfire, harlequin, flame) drive value. Check for doublet construction on the side.

provenance.abduction_hint: Iridescent cabochon = opal, subtype by body color and pattern.

messages.content: A brooch is a carved shell portrait in relief, oval frame. What are you looking at?

Shell cameo — most common cameo type.

Shell cameos are carved from mollusk shells, typically bull-mouth or queen conch. Hold to light — real shell is translucent, plastic is uniform. Age and quality of carving drive value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Relief-carved portrait in oval = cameo; material check separates shell from plastic.

messages.content: A brooch is a carved semi-precious stone portrait in relief, agate layered. What's this?

Hardstone cameo — often sardonyx or agate.

Hardstone cameos use layered agate or sardonyx where the carver exploits the color layers for the portrait. More valuable than shell. Depth of relief and quality of layering matter.

provenance.abduction_hint: Layered-stone portrait = hardstone cameo, premium tier.

messages.content: A ring with an intaglio carving — the design recessed into the stone. What's your read?

Intaglio ring — historical seal-ring convention or contemporary revival.

Intaglio is the reverse of cameo: the design is cut INTO the stone rather than in relief. Historically used for wax-seals. Contemporary artisan revival popular in men's jewelry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Recessed carving = intaglio; historically seal-ring, contemporary artisan revival.

messages.content: A brooch with a flat pin-back, hinged clasp, and safety catch — no rollover pin. Call it.

Modern brooch construction — post-1900.

Modern brooches have short pins with rotating C-clasps and safety catches. Older brooches (pre-1900) have long pins extending well past the frame, with simpler C-clasps.

provenance.abduction_hint: Modern short-pin with safety catch dates piece to 20th century onward.

messages.content: A brooch with a pin extending well past the frame edge, and a simple C-clasp with no safety catch. What are you looking at?

Antique brooch — pre-1900 construction, pin extended to secure through heavy Victorian fabric.

Long-pin brooches are pre-1900 because Victorian fabrics were heavy. The C-clasp without safety catch is period-correct. Handle with care — the pin can bend.

provenance.abduction_hint: Long-pin plus simple C-clasp = pre-1900 dating.

messages.content: A brooch with a trombone-style clasp (sliding tube). What's this?

European brooch, common 1890-1930.

Trombone clasps slide open like a trombone slide. European convention, common on Art Nouveau and early Art Deco brooches. Dates the piece to that window.

provenance.abduction_hint: Trombone-slide clasp = European 1890-1930 window.

messages.content: A brooch with a roll-over safety catch (a small metal ring that flips to secure the pin). What's your read?

Modern brooch, post-1930 convention.

Roll-over safety catches largely replaced simple C-clasps in the 20th century. Standard modern brooch construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Roll-over safety catch = 20th century post-1930.

messages.content: A brooch with a stem-and-lever safety catch. Call it.

Mid-century brooch, roughly 1940-1970 convention.

Stem-and-lever safety catches were popular mid-century. Less secure than modern flip catches but more secure than plain C-clasps. Dating hint but not definitive.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stem-and-lever catch = mid-20th-century window.

messages.content: A pair of earrings with wires that pass through the ear and bend back. What are you looking at?

French wire earrings — most common wire style, elegant, easy to lose.

French wires (also called shepherd hooks) are U-shaped wires with a bent-back end. Elegant but not the most secure. Adding a rubber stopper reduces loss.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bent-back U-shape wire = French wire construction.

messages.content: A pair of earrings with hinged wires that snap closed against a post. What's this?

Lever-back earrings — more secure than French wires.

Lever-backs have a hinged wire that snaps closed. Much more secure than French wires. Preferred for heavier or more valuable earrings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Snapping hinged wire = lever-back construction, better security.

messages.content: A pair of earrings with a very small round hoop and a hinged closure. What's your read?

Huggie earrings.

Huggies are tight small hoops with hinged closures. Sit close to the earlobe. Popular for everyday wear and for second/third piercings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Small tight hoop with hinge = huggie.

messages.content: A pair of earrings with a screw-thread post and a threaded back nut. Call it.

Screw-back earrings — high security.

Screw-back earrings have a threaded post and a matching nut that spins onto the thread. Very secure — used for valuable stones. Slower to put on than friction backs.

provenance.abduction_hint: Threaded post plus spinning nut = screw-back construction.

messages.content: A ring in an art-nouveau style — flowing organic lines, plique-à-jour enamel. What are you looking at?

Art Nouveau period (1890-1910) or contemporary revival.

Art Nouveau jewelry features flowing organic lines, natural motifs (dragonflies, wisteria), enamel, opal, moonstone. Peak period 1890-1910. Modern revivals exist — hallmarks and construction help date.

provenance.abduction_hint: Organic lines plus enamel plus period motifs = Art Nouveau family.

messages.content: A ring in Art Deco style — geometric, symmetric, contrasting stones. What's this?

Art Deco period (1920-1935) or contemporary revival.

Art Deco jewelry is geometric, often features platinum or white gold, contrasting stones (onyx-and-diamond, sapphire-and-diamond), calibré-cut stones, milgrain edges. Peak 1920-1935.

provenance.abduction_hint: Geometric symmetry plus period materials = Art Deco family.

messages.content: A ring in Victorian style — dark stones (jet, onyx), mourning motifs, hair-work. What's your read?

Victorian period (1837-1901) or contemporary mourning-revival.

Victorian jewelry spans a huge range but includes mourning jewelry with jet, onyx, hair-work, snake motifs (Victoria wore one), and heavy gold pieces. Sub-eras (Early, Grand, Late) differ.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mourning motifs plus dark stones plus period marks = Victorian.

messages.content: A ring in Edwardian style — filigree, milgrain, platinum-over-gold, pearls. Call it.

Edwardian period (1901-1915).

Edwardian jewelry features fine filigree, milgrain edges, platinum-topped-gold construction (Edwardian pieces often have gold under-carriage with platinum crown), and pearls. Delicate and lace-like.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fine filigree plus milgrain plus platinum-over-gold = Edwardian.

messages.content: A ring in Retro style — bold, oversized, rose gold, big citrine or aquamarine. What are you looking at?

Retro period (1935-1950).

Retro jewelry is bold and sculptural, often rose or yellow gold in large forms, big citrine, aquamarine, or synthetic stones (materials were scarce during WWII). Machine-age feel.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bold sculptural rose-gold with large stones = Retro period.

messages.content: A ring in Mid-Century Modern style — cluster settings, textured metal, colored stones. What's this?

Mid-Century Modern (1950-1970).

Mid-century jewelry features cluster settings, textured brushed gold, colored stones, atomic and floral motifs. American and Scandinavian traditions both prominent.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cluster settings plus textured metal = mid-century modern.

messages.content: A ring with heavy, minimalist geometric design in brushed silver. What's your read?

Brutalist or Modernist studio jewelry — 1960s-1970s.

Brutalist and modernist studio jewelry is heavy, minimalist, often brushed rather than polished, uses cast forms and simple geometric shapes. Studio makers signed. Look for maker mark.

provenance.abduction_hint: Heavy geometric brushed silver = Brutalist studio tradition.

messages.content: A cuff bracelet with hammered surface and a single large cabochon turquoise. Call it.

Southwest US Native or Native-style — often Navajo or Zuni tradition.

Southwest US Native jewelry commonly features hammered sterling and turquoise. Navajo tradition emphasizes silver work; Zuni emphasizes stonework and inlay. Look for artist signatures — Native makers often sign.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hammered sterling plus cabochon turquoise plus cuff form = Southwest tradition.

messages.content: A ring with small stones set in a raised silverwork pattern with fine granulation. What are you looking at?

Bali silver tradition or similar Indonesian studio work.

Bali silver features granulation, twisted wire, and detailed stone-setting on sterling bases. Handmade, sometimes signed. Common motifs: floral, geometric, mystical.

provenance.abduction_hint: Granulation plus twisted wire plus Indonesian style = Bali tradition.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'K18' (K before number, not after). What's this?

18-karat gold, Japanese convention.

Japanese jewelry marks put K before the karat number. Same meaning as 18K in US convention. Signals Japanese origin — Japanese craftsmanship often high-quality.

provenance.abduction_hint: K-before-number mark = Japanese convention.

messages.content: A ring stamped '333' and 'GERMANY'. What's your read?

8-karat gold, German convention. Very low karat — sometimes not considered 'gold' in US jurisdiction.

333 = 8K (33.3% gold). Legal to sell as gold in Germany and much of Europe. Not US-legal as 'gold'. Older European standard, still common on antique pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Low European fineness = non-US-market gold convention.

messages.content: A ring stamped '375'. Call it.

9-karat gold — British minimum-legal (37.5%).

375 = 9K British standard. Common on UK, Australian, and NZ jewelry. Slightly less warm-yellow due to higher copper content. Legal as 'gold' in Commonwealth, not in US.

provenance.abduction_hint: 375 = British/Commonwealth 9K convention.

messages.content: A ring stamped '417'. What are you looking at?

10-karat gold — US minimum (41.7%).

417 = 10K US convention expressed per-thousand. Common on modern US pieces, especially those marketed internationally where numeric marks are preferred.

provenance.abduction_hint: 417 = 10K US-standard low karat, most durable karat.

messages.content: A ring stamped '833' — silver piece. What's this?

Silver at 83.3% purity — Dutch or European convention.

833 silver was a Dutch and European standard, common on 19th-century pieces. Below sterling but legally silver in Netherlands historically.

provenance.abduction_hint: 833 = Dutch/European silver convention, historical.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'RUSSIA' and '875'. What's your read?

Russian silver at 87.5% purity — Soviet-era convention.

875 was the Soviet-era Russian silver standard. Common on 20th-century Russian pieces. Slightly below sterling but legally silver in USSR/Russia.

provenance.abduction_hint: Russian origin plus 875 = Soviet-era silver convention.

messages.content: A ring, silver-toned, stamped 'MEXICO' and 'TS-101' inside the band. Call it.

Mexican sterling with a Taxco maker code — TS marks identify Taxco makers by numeric code.

Taxco silver marks include TA, TC, TE, TF, TG, TH, TI, TJ, TK, TL, TM, TN, TO, TP, TQ, TR, TS, TT, TU, TV, TW, TX, TY, TZ followed by numbers, uniquely identifying the maker. Some are collectible.

provenance.abduction_hint: TS-plus-number in Mexican silver = Taxco maker code, lookup for provenance.

messages.content: A ring with intricately-detailed silver work and a small stamp of a Fu-dog or dragon. What are you looking at?

Chinese studio silver or export silver — the mythological creature marks the tradition.

Chinese export silver marks vary widely — pseudo-hallmarks were common. Fu-dog, dragon, and phoenix marks appear on both genuine studio pieces and export-market pieces. Style, weight, and workmanship help distinguish.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mythological creature stamp on Chinese-style silver = tradition marker, tier by workmanship.

messages.content: A ring with heavy filigree and colored enamel work, marked '925' with a bee symbol. What's this?

Portuguese silver — bee is a common Portuguese assay-house mark.

Portuguese silver hallmarks include the bee, boar's head, and other symbols. Portugal has strong filigree and enamel traditions. Sometimes very high-quality.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bee mark plus 925 on filigree-and-enamel piece = Portuguese tradition.

messages.content: A ring with a black stone that has slight red glints under angled light. What's your read?

Possibly garnet (dark red almandine can appear black in low light) or possibly black spinel.

Dark almandine garnet often appears near-black but shows deep red under angled light. Black spinel is truly black and doesn't show red. Cut and setting also help — antique pieces often used almandine cabochons.

provenance.abduction_hint: Black stone with red glints on angle = garnet family; solid black = spinel or onyx.

messages.content: A ring with an orange-red stone showing exceptional brilliance and no visible inclusions. Call it.

Possibly spessartine garnet (orange), padparadscha sapphire, or lab-grown alternative.

Rich orange-red gems include spessartine and mandarin garnet, padparadscha sapphire (extremely rare, valuable), imperial topaz, and modern lab-grown alternatives. Certification recommended for higher-value stones.

provenance.abduction_hint: Rich orange-red with brilliance = premium orange-family gem, verify identity.

messages.content: A ring with a stone that shows blue-and-red color-shift at different angles. What are you looking at?

Possibly tanzanite (blue-purple pleochroism) or possibly iolite.

Tanzanite is famously pleochroic — showing blue and purple from different crystal angles. Iolite (dichroite) does similarly. Cutter chooses which color faces up. Distinct signature of pleochroic stones.

provenance.abduction_hint: Directional color change (not lighting-based) = pleochroism, narrows stone type.

messages.content: A ring with a pink stone that shows lighter pink at edges and deeper pink in center. What's this?

Consistent with morganite (pink beryl) or pink sapphire — color zoning typical.

Morganite is pink beryl, cut in cushion or oval, popular in rose-gold settings. Pink sapphires are harder (Mohs 9 vs 7.5-8 for morganite) and pricier. Color zoning is normal in both.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pink with center-heavy saturation = morganite or pink sapphire, hardness resolves.

messages.content: A ring with a stone showing pale yellow body and rainbow-fire flash from within. What's your read?

Possibly heliodor (yellow beryl), yellow sapphire, or citrine — all in the pale-yellow range.

Yellow gemstones include heliodor, yellow sapphire, citrine (yellow quartz), yellow diamond, and imperial topaz. Testing (specific gravity, refractive index) separates. Citrine is common, cheap; yellow diamond and yellow sapphire are premium.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pale yellow with fire = yellow-gem family, testing needed for identity.

messages.content: A ring with a very pale purple stone that fades in strong sunlight over hours. Call it.

Possibly amethyst (light-sensitive) or synthetic sapphire — natural amethyst fades under prolonged sunlight.

Amethyst can fade if exposed to direct sunlight for extended periods. Some heat-treated amethyst is particularly light-sensitive. Store in dark to preserve color. Synthetic sapphires generally do not fade.

provenance.abduction_hint: Light-sensitive purple = amethyst family, storage advice.

messages.content: A ring with a stone that shows a milky sheen — silver-blue over white. What are you looking at?

Moonstone (adularescence) — the blue sheen floats over the stone body.

Adularescence is moonstone's signature optical effect — a blue or white sheen that floats and moves with the light. Best moonstones show blue sheen; whites are common and cheaper. Cabochon cut always.

provenance.abduction_hint: Blue-white floating sheen = adularescence = moonstone.

messages.content: A ring with a stone that shows a metallic-golden sheen against dark body. What's this?

Labradorite (labradorescence) — golden or blue metallic sheen against gray body.

Labradorite's labradorescence is a metallic sheen — usually blue, green, or gold — that flashes at specific angles. High-flash pieces are called spectrolite. Cabochon cut required.

provenance.abduction_hint: Metallic angular flash on gray stone = labradorescence = labradorite.

messages.content: A ring with a stone showing red-and-green flashes in a dark-body cabochon. What's your read?

Fire opal or precious black opal — play-of-color with warm hues.

Fire opal has a warm body color (orange or red) with or without play-of-color. When it also has play, called precious fire opal. Very desirable. Body color and play both drive value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Warm-hue play-of-color = fire opal family.

messages.content: A ring with a stone showing green-body and needle-like red inclusions. Call it.

Possibly demantoid garnet — the 'horsetail' inclusions are diagnostic.

Demantoid garnet is a rare green garnet variety. The 'horsetail' inclusions (branching fibrous crystals of chrysotile) are diagnostic and INCREASE value — a rare case where inclusions are prized. Russian demantoid is the collector's grade.

provenance.abduction_hint: Horsetail inclusions in green stone = demantoid, inclusions ADD value.

messages.content: A ring with two silver-toned rings soldered together at a fixed angle. What are you looking at?

Toi et moi ring — two-stone or two-band construction, French sentimental tradition.

'Toi et moi' (you and me) rings feature two stones or two bands representing a pair. French sentimental tradition, revived in modern engagement designs. Distinct from three-stone rings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-stone paired construction with angled setting = toi et moi.

messages.content: A ring with three interlocking bands that can be pushed together to form one ring. What's this?

Puzzle ring or Turkish wedding ring — traditional four-band interlocking construction (or three-band variant).

Puzzle rings are 3-band, 4-band, or more, interlocking such that removing one and reassembling requires knowing the pattern. Historical Middle-Eastern tradition (used to test bride's cleverness). Contemporary revival popular.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multiple interlocking bands that separate = puzzle ring construction.

messages.content: A ring with a stone that shifts from green to red under different lighting. What's your read?

Alexandrite (or lab-grown alexandrite / color-change garnet).

Natural alexandrite shifts from green (daylight) to red (incandescent). Lab-grown alexandrite exhibits the same shift and is a fraction of the price. Certificate required to confirm natural.

provenance.abduction_hint: Green-to-red color shift = alexandrite or synthetic alternative.

messages.content: A ring with a stone that shows two colors depending on crystal orientation. Call it.

Pleochroic stone — tanzanite, iolite, tourmaline, or andalusite candidates.

Pleochroism is a natural property of anisotropic crystals — different colors from different crystal directions. Cutters choose orientation for the most desirable face-up color. Testing distinguishes species.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-color from angle change = pleochroism, species TBD.

messages.content: A ring with a pearl that shifts iridescent colors across its surface. What are you looking at?

Peacock pearl or high-quality Tahitian pearl.

Peacock and Tahitian pearls show iridescent overtones — green, blue, purple — over their body color. Round Tahitian pearls with high overtone are premium; drop and baroque shapes are also collector-grade.

provenance.abduction_hint: Iridescent overtones on dark pearl = Tahitian, peacock overtone premium.

messages.content: A necklace of large uniform white pearls, each slightly asymmetric, warm luster. What's this?

Likely South Sea pearls — largest cultured pearls, warm creamy luster.

South Sea pearls (from the Pinctada maxima oyster) are the largest cultured pearls, typically 10-15mm. Warm creamy white to golden. High value. Slight asymmetry is normal even in high grades.

provenance.abduction_hint: Large white pearls with warm luster = South Sea family.

messages.content: A necklace of tiny very-round white pearls, high mirror-luster. What's your read?

Akoya pearls — the classic small round cultured white pearl.

Akoya pearls (from Pinctada fucata) are the classic pearl-strand pearl. Small (6-9mm typical), very round, mirror-luster. Japanese and Chinese production dominates.

provenance.abduction_hint: Small round mirror-luster white pearls = Akoya family.

messages.content: A ring with a pearl set on a metal cup with a peg glued into the drilled hole. Call it.

Pearl-on-post construction — standard for pearl rings, watch for glue integrity.

Pearl rings typically use a metal cup with a peg glued through a drilled pearl. Glue can weaken over time — check for looseness. Some pearls are only half-drilled (glued) or fully drilled (threaded through).

provenance.abduction_hint: Cup-and-peg construction = standard pearl-mount, inspect glue.

messages.content: An estate-sale box contains a jumble of forty rings. You have six minutes before the next buyer arrives. What are you looking at?

Sort in two passes: first, magnet-test everything to eliminate steel and nickel plated; second, group the survivors by mark — 925/STER together, K-marks together, unmarked heavy pieces together, unmarked light pieces to the side. Take the K-marks and any heavy unmarked pieces for further evaluation.

Estate-sale triage is exactly what a Kellman glance trains you for. Two-pass sorting compresses 40 items to a manageable subset in the time available. Magnet-plus-mark is the fastest coarse sort.

provenance.abduction_hint: Time-constrained scan compresses decisions to two attributes: magnetism (metal type gate) plus mark presence (value gate).

messages.content: A ring stamped '18K' has a green tinge showing under the yellow-gold surface, visible only in a magnified inspection of the underside. What's this?

Solid 18K green-gold alloy, not plating. The green shows through because the alloy IS greenish — silver-and-cadmium alloyed with 75% gold.

Green gold is genuine solid gold with a silver-rich alloy composition. Novices often mistake the greenish undertone for plating wear. The mark is the source of truth: 18K stamped means solid.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mark plus alloy-color plus consistent color throughout = solid alloy, not plating.

messages.content: A necklace clasp with '925' is attached to a chain that shows the base metal at every wear point. What's your read?

Sterling-silver clasp on a silver-plated base-metal chain — a common bait-and-switch construction.

Sellers sometimes attach a genuine sterling clasp (which carries the required legal mark) to a plated chain to avoid marking-law issues. The mark applies only to the clasp; the chain itself is not silver. Buyer should be told.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mark on component vs mark on whole is legally distinct — one-component mark does not authenticate the piece.

messages.content: A ring with a large center diamond has small holes drilled through the pavilion facets. Call it.

Laser-drilled diamond — likely inclusions were laser-drilled to remove or reduce dark inclusions.

Laser drilling is a common diamond enhancement — a fine channel is drilled to an inclusion to allow acid to bleach it. Enhancement must be disclosed. The drill channels are visible under a loupe as very fine straight lines. Value is reduced vs an equivalent-clarity untreated stone.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fine straight-line features inside diamond = laser-drilling enhancement, disclose.

messages.content: A ring with an emerald has visible oiling residue at surface-reaching inclusions. What are you looking at?

Oil-treated emerald — nearly all emeralds are oil-treated to fill surface-reaching fractures. Disclosure required.

Oiling is the most common emerald treatment — cedar oil, canada balsam, or resin fills surface fractures to improve apparent clarity. Cleaning ruins the treatment. Untreated emeralds command a premium. Type of oil (traditional, modern resin) affects durability.

provenance.abduction_hint: Residue in surface fractures of emerald = oil treatment, standard practice, disclose.

messages.content: A brooch stamped '750' has a small mark showing a woman's head in profile. What's this?

French 18K gold. The Marianne's head is the French assay-office mark for imported gold; the eagle's head is the French mark for domestic 18K.

French hallmarks are complex — the head symbols distinguish domestic/imported and karat. Eagle head = French 18K domestic; Marianne head = imported 18K; horse head = imported 14K. Detailed reference books cover full systems.

provenance.abduction_hint: Head symbols in French marks = assay-office marks, category by species.

messages.content: A ring with an aquamarine has slightly greenish tone. Under filtered light the color deepens to blue. What's your read?

Untreated aquamarine — natural color often has green tone; heat treatment removes the green to give pure blue.

Most commercial aquamarine is heat-treated to remove greenish tones and produce pure blue. Untreated stones with visible green tone are increasingly valued by collectors who prefer natural over treated.

provenance.abduction_hint: Greenish tone in aquamarine = untreated, collector-valued naturalness.

messages.content: A ring with a tanzanite has intense blue with no violet showing face-up. Call it.

Heat-treated tanzanite — natural tanzanite is more brown-purple; heat treatment (about 500°C) shifts it to the desired violet-blue.

Nearly all commercial tanzanite is heat-treated — the color shift is dramatic and permanent. Untreated tanzanite is rare and collector-graded. Standard practice, disclosure required but often minimal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Intense blue tanzanite = heat-treated, expected, disclose.

messages.content: A ring with a large golden sapphire, no visible inclusions, glowing color. What are you looking at?

Possibly beryllium-diffusion-treated sapphire — a modern treatment that alters color to yellow/orange/pink.

Beryllium diffusion is a modern treatment that dramatically alters sapphire color from pale or off-color to intense yellow, orange, or pink (padparadscha imitation). Legally must be disclosed. Value reduced vs untreated. Certification distinguishes.

provenance.abduction_hint: Suspiciously intense uniform color in sapphire = diffusion candidate.

messages.content: A ring with a large diamond that shows blue fluorescence under UV lamp. What's this?

Fluorescent diamond — 25-35% of diamonds show blue fluorescence under longwave UV. Slight discount in market pricing but not a defect.

Blue fluorescence can slightly mask yellow tint in a diamond (masking a Z-M range down to look H-J) but strong fluorescence in colorless stones causes 'milky' appearance in sunlight. Market discount is 5-15% for strong fluorescence in top color-grade stones.

provenance.abduction_hint: UV blue-glow in diamond = fluorescence, standard variable, market factor.

messages.content: A ring with an old-mine-cut diamond has soft warm sparkle vs the crisp bright sparkle of a modern brilliant. What's your read?

Old-mine-cut sparkle character — hand-cut for candlelight, not electric light. The 'romantic' sparkle.

Old-cuts were designed before electric lighting; they scatter and warm the light rather than throwing bright pinpoints. Collectors value the romantic character. Vintage market prices old-cuts by carat plus 'antique premium.'

provenance.abduction_hint: Soft warm scattered sparkle vs bright pinpoint sparkle = old-cut vs modern-cut character.

messages.content: A ring with an emerald mounted in yellow gold, and the piece has 'MADE IN COLOMBIA' engraved inside the band. Call it.

Colombian emerald origin claim — Muzo, Chivor, or Coscuez mines historically produce the finest emeralds.

Origin markers on emerald pieces add value if genuine — Colombian emeralds command premium over Zambian or Brazilian. Origin cannot be conclusively determined without laboratory analysis, so 'MADE IN COLOMBIA' engravings are seller claims, not verifications.

provenance.abduction_hint: Origin claim in engraving = value-boosting claim, verify with lab for high-value pieces.

messages.content: A ring with a pearl that shows tiny concentric circular patterns on the surface under magnification. What are you looking at?

Consistent with natural saltwater pearl (Akoya, South Sea) — the concentric patterns are nacre growth rings.

Nacre grows in concentric layers; the growth pattern is visible under magnification. Imitation pearls have painted or coated surfaces with no such pattern. Combined with tooth-test grit, definitively real.

provenance.abduction_hint: Growth-ring surface pattern under magnification = organic origin.

messages.content: A ring in Georgian style with a foil-backed rose-cut diamond in a closed-back setting. What's this?

Georgian jewelry (1714-1837) — foil-backing was standard to enhance stone color; closed-back settings are period-correct.

Georgian jewelry rarely comes to market. Foil-backing (metal foil behind the stone to reflect light and add color) is period-correct but limits modern cleaning. Closed backs (no light entry from below) are period-standard. Verify age with construction and hallmark research.

provenance.abduction_hint: Foil-backing plus closed-back = pre-1837 Georgian tradition.

messages.content: A ring in Art Deco style with calibré-cut sapphires — small custom-shaped stones fitted to the setting. What's your read?

Art Deco period (1920-1935) — calibré-cutting was a signature of the period.

Calibré-cut stones are cut to fit a specific setting — trapezoidal, half-moon, custom shapes. Labor-intensive. Signature of Art Deco period. Modern jewelry rarely uses calibré. High-quality Deco pieces command premium.

provenance.abduction_hint: Custom-shaped small stones fitted to setting = calibré cutting, Art Deco signature.

messages.content: A ring stamped 'PLAT' with a broken prong on a solitaire — the metal is torn, not bent. Call it.

Genuine platinum — platinum tears rather than bends when severely stressed, because of its work-hardening behavior.

Platinum's mechanical behavior — it work-hardens and tears rather than bending easily — is a subtle indicator of authenticity. White-gold prongs bend; platinum prongs tear or break cleanly. Not a primary test but a corroborating observation.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mechanical failure mode plus mark = corroborating authentication.

messages.content: A ring with an emerald has 'no oil' certificate from GIA. What are you looking at?

Untreated emerald with laboratory certification — premium value, rare in commercial market.

GIA and other major labs certify treatment status. 'No oil' or 'no treatment' certifications on emerald are rare and command significant premium. Verify certificate number against issuing lab's database.

provenance.abduction_hint: Untreated-emerald certificate = rare-tier authentication, premium pricing.

messages.content: A ring in Retro style with a large aquamarine set in rose gold, alongside small ruby accents. What's this?

Retro period (1935-1950) — bold aquamarine plus rose-gold-plus-ruby was a signature combination during WWII material scarcity.

Retro-period jewelry often paired big colored stones (aquamarine, citrine) with rose gold and small red accents (ruby, garnet, or synthetic). Materials were scarce; makers designed for visual impact with less precious content.

provenance.abduction_hint: Aquamarine plus rose gold plus red accents = Retro-period signature combination.

messages.content: A ring with three fine gold bands soldered together and stamped '15K' with a British hallmark. What's your read?
