provenance.abduction_hint: Italian silk tie = quality convention, verify by construction.

messages.content: A tie label reads 'Hand-Rolled.' Call it.

Higher-quality tie construction. Hand-rolled edges have softer drape than machine-sewn edges. Marker of tier.

Hand-rolled tie edges are a traditional quality marker. Softer roll, better drape, less bulk. Machine-rolled is functional; hand-rolled is craft-tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-rolled edges = craft-tier tie construction.

messages.content: A tie label reads '7-Fold.' What are you looking at?

Premium tie construction — silk folded 7 times to make the tie body without interlining. Heaviest, most luxurious tier.

7-fold ties use only silk, folded 7 times to form the tie body — no interlining. Heaviest, thickest, most luxurious tier. 3-fold or 4-fold with interlining is standard.

provenance.abduction_hint: 7-fold tie = premium construction, no interlining.

messages.content: A T-shirt has a printed graphic that has cracked along fold lines. What's this?

Print aging or heavy wear. Printed graphics crack over years of washing. Cannot be repaired; part of used-T-shirt aesthetic for some.

Printed T-shirt graphics crack with washing and wear. Vintage T-shirt collectors sometimes value the cracking as authenticity marker. Modern buyers usually see it as damage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Print cracking = wear aging, aesthetic-dependent value.

messages.content: A hoodie has extreme pilling in the underarm area. What's your read?

Wear pattern. Underarm pilling is a high-friction wear point. Removable with pilling shaver; cosmetic only.

Underarm pilling is common on hoodies and sweaters. Pilling shavers restore appearance. Doesn't indicate garment failure; long-staple fibers pill less over life.

provenance.abduction_hint: Underarm pilling = friction wear, cosmetic.

messages.content: A dress shirt has a discreet contrasting-color thread stitched into the collar underside. Call it.

Brand-signature detail. Some brands use hidden contrasting-color stitching as a subtle marker. Verify against reference photos for brand.

Brand-signature details (contrasting-thread stitching, small labels, gauntlet button details) are subtle authentication markers. Not universal; brand-specific. Reference photos help identify.

provenance.abduction_hint: Signature detail = brand marker, verify against reference.

messages.content: A wool sweater label reads '100% Cashmere — Made in China.' What are you looking at?

Chinese-made cashmere. Not automatically lower quality; some fine Chinese cashmere. Verify by hand feel and pilling test.

China produces cashmere at all quality tiers — from mass-market blended-and-mislabeled to fine studio-grade. Quality within Chinese cashmere varies enormously. Feel and label together.

provenance.abduction_hint: Country + material combination = one signal, not full picture.

messages.content: A pair of shoes has visible glue seepage around the sole-upper joint. What's this?

Cemented (glued) construction. Standard on affordable footwear. Cannot be resoled economically.

Glue seepage indicates cemented sole. Standard on inexpensive shoes. Cannot be resoled. Life ends when sole wears or separates.

provenance.abduction_hint: Glue at sole joint = cemented construction.

messages.content: A pair of shoes has a visible ridge of stitching around the perimeter where sole meets upper. What's your read?

Goodyear welt construction. Repairable — resolable multiple times. Higher tier shoe.

Goodyear welt = strip of leather ('welt') stitched between upper and outsole, allowing sole replacement without disturbing upper. Highest tier construction. Restorable multiple times.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible perimeter stitching + welt = Goodyear welt construction.

messages.content: A pair of shoes has stitching hidden between sole layers. Call it.

Blake stitch construction. Sole-through-insole stitching. More flexible, less waterproof than Goodyear welt. Repairable.

Blake stitch runs through insole and sole. Flexible, thinner shoe profile. Repairable (special Blake stitch machine required at cobbler). More common in Italian shoemaking.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hidden stitching between soles = Blake stitch, Italian tradition.

messages.content: A handbag has hardware (buckles, closures) that magnet-sticks. What are you looking at?

Steel or steel-plated hardware. Common on mid-tier bags. Brass or genuine metal is non-magnetic.

Higher-tier handbags use solid brass hardware (non-magnetic). Mid-tier uses steel or steel with plating (magnetic). Not always a value differentiator; some brands use both.

provenance.abduction_hint: Magnetic hardware = steel-based, various tiers.

messages.content: A dress shirt has a placket that is fabric-covered. What's this?

Fly-front (French placket) shirt. Formal-style. Buttons hidden behind fabric flap. Higher formality tier.

Fly-front (concealed placket) shirts hide the button placket behind a fabric flap. Formal-style, typical of dress shirts. More common on modern shirts than vintage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Concealed placket = formal shirt style.

messages.content: A dress shirt has a placket with visible buttons but no separate fabric flap. What's your read?

Standard front-placket shirt. Casual to business style. Buttons visible.

Standard front placket has buttons visible. Casual to business shirts. Fabric flap either present (French placket) or absent (visible buttons). Standard style.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible buttons + no flap = standard placket.

messages.content: A wool coat lining shows a printed brand pattern repeated. Call it.

Branded lining pattern. Verify pattern accuracy against reference. Counterfeit-friendly detail — genuine pattern is precise.

Branded lining patterns (e.g., house tartan for coat brands) are counterfeited. Genuine patterns match brand specifications precisely — repeat, colors, scale. Verify.

provenance.abduction_hint: Branded lining = verify against reference.

messages.content: A jacket lapel is exactly 3.5 inches wide. What are you looking at?

Standard classic lapel width. Timeless proportions, not fashion-era-specific.

Lapel widths trend with fashion eras. 3-3.5 inches = classic. Under 2.5 inches = skinny (2005-2015 trend). Over 4 inches = 1970s or wide-lapel trend (2019-onward).

provenance.abduction_hint: Standard lapel width = classic, era-neutral.

messages.content: A jacket lapel is 2 inches wide. What's this?

Skinny lapel — 2005-2015 fashion era. Now dated. Priced accordingly on resale.

Skinny lapels peaked around 2010. Current fashion (2020s) has returned to wider lapels. Skinny-lapel suits look dated; resale value affected. Some buyers still prefer for their aesthetic.

provenance.abduction_hint: Very narrow lapel = 2005-2015 era.

messages.content: A jacket lapel is 4.5 inches wide. What's your read?

Wide lapel — 1970s-1980s or modern wide-lapel revival (2019+).

Wide lapels peaked in 1970s. Currently returning to fashion (2019+ trend). Dating by lapel width requires cross-reference to other era markers (fabric, cut, construction).

provenance.abduction_hint: Wide lapel = 1970s or modern revival, cross-check era.

messages.content: A jacket has notched lapels. Call it.

Notch lapel — most common lapel style. Casual to business formal.

Notch lapels are the standard for business suits, sport coats, and blazers. Not formal enough for tuxedos (which use peak or shawl). Timeless choice.

provenance.abduction_hint: Notch lapel = standard business/casual style.

messages.content: A jacket has peaked lapels pointing sharply upward. What are you looking at?

Peak lapel — formal or slightly more elegant business style. Tuxedos, double-breasted jackets.

Peak lapels are more formal than notch — traditional on tuxedos and double-breasted suits. Modern single-breasted suits with peak lapels are a slight upgrade from notch styling.

provenance.abduction_hint: Peak lapel = formal/elegant tier.

messages.content: A jacket has shawl lapels — smooth curve, no notch or peak. What's this?

Shawl lapel — formal, typical of tuxedos and smoking jackets.

Shawl collar/lapel is a smooth continuous curve. Traditional for tuxedos, smoking jackets, and dinner jackets. Elegant, evening-wear-focused.

provenance.abduction_hint: Shawl lapel = formal evening-wear style.

messages.content: A jacket buttons open double-breasted (6-on-2 pattern). What's your read?

Double-breasted jacket. Classic 6x2 button pattern common. More formal than single-breasted; British/naval-influenced.

Double-breasted button patterns: 6x2 (six buttons total, two-button closure), 6x1, 4x1, 4x2. Higher formality than single-breasted. Currently trending after 2015+.

provenance.abduction_hint: Double-breasted = formal/traditional style.

messages.content: A wool suit has a slight sheen when tilted under light. Call it.

Worsted wool. Standard business suit fabric. Sheen from tight worsted weave.

Worsted wool has slight sheen from tightly-twisted long-staple wool yarns. Standard business suit fabric. Warmer worsted has less sheen; lightweight has more.

provenance.abduction_hint: Wool + slight sheen = worsted, business suit.

messages.content: A wool suit has visible slubs and rough texture. What are you looking at?

Woolen (as opposed to worsted). Rougher texture, more casual. Tweed and flannel are examples.

Woolen (woolen-spun) uses shorter staple wool, resulting in rougher texture. Not less warm; often warmer. Common in tweeds, flannels, and country cloths.

provenance.abduction_hint: Rough-textured wool = woolen-spun (vs worsted-spun).

messages.content: A shirt has visible pinstripes in a very fine pattern. What's this?

Pinstripe shirt. Common formal-business fabric. Distinctive of banker-tradition.

Pinstripe patterns come in many scales. Fine (banker) pinstripes are subtle; chalk stripes are wider and often on darker fabric. Both formal-business tradition.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fine pinstripes = banker-tradition formal shirt.

messages.content: A wool label reads 'Super 120s.' What's your read?

Wool fiber fineness — 120s = 18.75 micron average. Fine tier suitable for lightweight fine suits.

Super numbers grade wool fineness. Super 100s = 19.5 micron. Super 120s = 18.75. Super 150s = 17.5. Super 180s = 16.5. Higher numbers = finer + softer + more delicate. Everyday wear tier stops around Super 130s.

provenance.abduction_hint: Super number = wool fineness tier.

messages.content: A wool label reads 'Super 180s.' Call it.

Very fine wool — 16.5 micron. Delicate. High-end tier but wears out faster than lower super numbers.

Super 180s is very fine wool — luxurious hand but delicate. Wrinkles more, wears faster, requires careful maintenance. Ideal for special occasions rather than daily wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Very high super number = luxury tier but delicate.

messages.content: A jacket label reads 'Loro Piana Fabric.' What are you looking at?

Premium Italian wool mill fabric. Not brand of jacket, but fabric supplier. Adds to value if the jacket is well-constructed.

Loro Piana is a premium Italian wool mill (also a garment brand). Their fabrics are used by many maker-brands. Combined with premium construction = highest tier. Fabric alone doesn't authenticate the whole garment.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fabric mill = tier signal, verify construction independently.

messages.content: A cashmere label reads '2-ply.' What's this?

Cashmere ply count — 2 yarns twisted together. Stronger, more durable than single-ply. Standard for quality knitwear.

Cashmere ply count: single-ply (loose weave, light), 2-ply (standard quality), 4-ply and higher (heavier, more durable, luxury). Higher ply = better durability.

provenance.abduction_hint: 2-ply cashmere = standard quality tier.

messages.content: A cashmere label reads '10-ply.' What's your read?

Very heavy cashmere knit. Luxury tier — dense, warm, expensive. Rare in most price ranges.

10-ply cashmere is extremely luxurious — dense knit, exceptional warmth and hand. Correspondingly heavy and expensive. Rare in commercial market outside luxury brands.

provenance.abduction_hint: 10-ply cashmere = ultra-luxury tier.

messages.content: A jacket label reads 'Made in Italy, Naples region.' Call it.

Neapolitan tailoring tradition. Soft-shouldered construction, minimal padding. Traditional Neapolitan makers include several premium tailoring houses.

Neapolitan tailoring is a specific tradition — soft shoulders, unstructured chest, comfortable drape. Distinct from British (structured), American (moderate structure). Some premium tailoring houses base in Naples region.

provenance.abduction_hint: Naples region tailoring = Neapolitan soft-shoulder tradition.

messages.content: A jacket has minimal shoulder padding — soft draped shoulder line. What are you looking at?

Neapolitan or unstructured tailoring. Casual to smart-casual style. Comfortable to wear; less formal appearance.

Shoulder structure: British (heavily structured, pronounced shoulder), American (moderate), Neapolitan (unstructured, soft). Choice affects formality and comfort. Modern casual trend favors softer shoulders.

provenance.abduction_hint: Soft/unstructured shoulder = casual or Neapolitan tradition.

messages.content: A jacket has heavily structured shoulders with distinct roped seam at sleeve head. What's this?

British/Savile Row-influenced tailoring. Formal, structured, traditional. Higher formality than soft-shouldered.

Roped shoulder (with pronounced seam ridge) is British tradition. Structured chest, padded shoulders. Formal traditional style. Suitable for formal occasions and city business dress.

provenance.abduction_hint: Structured/roped shoulder = British formal tradition.

messages.content: A jacket collar shows a small triangle of contrasting fabric visible at the collar underside. What's your read?

Boutonnière loop or brand-marker detail. Some brands use color-marker as authenticity signal.

Some tailoring brands include distinct color-marker details as brand signatures — boutonnière loops, contrasting-thread stitches, small labels. Verify against brand references.

provenance.abduction_hint: Contrasting-color detail = brand marker or utility (boutonnière loop).

messages.content: A trouser has a Milanese buttonhole (rolled edge, keyhole shape). Call it.

Traditional high-quality buttonhole style. Rolled edge, keyhole (round + slit). Modernized in some contemporary tailoring.

Milanese buttonhole is a specific traditional style — rolled edge, keyhole shape. Hand-worked traditionally; machine-worked in modern equivalents. Higher quality garment marker.

provenance.abduction_hint: Milanese buttonhole = traditional high-quality construction.

messages.content: A suit has one working buttonhole on the lapel but the button is missing. What are you looking at?

Buttonhole for boutonnière (flower). Traditional detail. Genuine hand-worked buttonhole with rolled edge is a quality marker.

The lapel buttonhole is for a boutonnière (flower). Traditional formal detail. Genuine hand-worked buttonhole is a quality marker. Function still valid — decorative or ceremonial.

provenance.abduction_hint: Lapel buttonhole = boutonnière detail, quality marker.

messages.content: A jacket has a full-canvas construction claim, but the chest feels flat and boardlike when pressed. What's this?

Suspicious — full canvas feels supple and molded. Boardlike feel suggests fused construction. Verify with additional inspection.

Full canvas construction has a specific supple, three-dimensional feel. Fused chest is flat and boardlike. If claim doesn't match feel, one is wrong. Feel wins.

provenance.abduction_hint: Chest feel vs canvas-claim mismatch = investigate.

messages.content: A shirt collar interlining is soft and drapes naturally when unbuttoned. What's your read?

Unfused (soft) collar. Higher quality construction. Better drape than fused.

Unfused (soft, unfused) collars use woven interlining without adhesive. Natural drape, ages well, no bubbling. Higher-tier shirt construction. Mass-market shirts almost always fused.

provenance.abduction_hint: Soft collar drape = unfused construction, quality tier.

messages.content: A silk tie has a diagonal repeat pattern of small emblems. Call it.

Regimental or club tie. Pattern may indicate specific school, military unit, club. Not appropriate to wear without membership in some traditions.

Regimental ties have specific patterns tied to schools, military units, clubs. Traditional English etiquette says wear only ties from clubs you're a member of. Some patterns are open (no meaning); others are strictly associated.

provenance.abduction_hint: Regimental pattern = affiliation-tied, verify meaning.

messages.content: A leather jacket has visible pinhole pattern along all edges. What are you looking at?

Perforated leather ventilation. Common on motorcycle jackets, some casual leather jackets.

Perforated leather (small pinholes) provides ventilation in warm weather. Common on motorcycle jackets, some casual designs. Distinct look; not damage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform perforation = intentional ventilation.

messages.content: A shoe upper has small stitched wingtip pattern with pinhole punching. What's this?

Brogue shoe. Traditional Scottish/Irish origin (originally for water drainage in bog work). Now standard dress shoe style.

Brogues have punched hole patterns on the upper. Full brogue = wingtip with punching. Semi-brogue = simpler wingtip. Longwing = wingtip extending back to heel. Broguing was originally functional (water drainage) now decorative.

provenance.abduction_hint: Punched hole pattern on shoe = brogue style, traditional.

messages.content: A shoe has visible stitching where sole meets upper — thicker leather layer between them. What's your read?

Goodyear welt construction. Highest tier stitched-sole construction. Resolable multiple times.

Goodyear welt uses a leather strip (welt) sewn between upper and insole, then to outsole. Sole can be replaced by removing bottom stitching without disturbing upper. Highest quality tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible welt + double stitching = Goodyear construction.

messages.content: A dress shoe has a smooth stitchless-appearing sole-upper joint. Call it.

Cemented (glued) construction. Cannot resole. Standard on affordable and some fashion-oriented dress shoes.

Cemented shoes have glue-bonded soles. Cannot be economically resoled. Standard on affordable footwear and many modern dress shoes. Aesthetically clean; short life.

provenance.abduction_hint: Smooth stitchless joint = cemented construction.

messages.content: A dress shoe has a stitched sole-upper joint plus stitching visible on the sole running around the perimeter. What are you looking at?

Goodyear welt with visible outsole stitching. Traditional formal shoe construction. Very durable.

Goodyear welt with visible outsole stitching is the traditional formal shoe construction. Extremely durable. Repairable multiple times. Higher weight than cemented shoes.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-line stitching around perimeter = Goodyear welt.

messages.content: A handbag interior has a small square metal placard reading 'MADE IN FRANCE — DATE CODE C.' What's this?

French-made handbag with date code. Verify authenticity — date code correlates to year of manufacture in many premium brand databases.

Premium handbag brands use date codes to track manufacturing year. Verify code format matches brand's system. Codes are counterfeited but often incorrectly (wrong format, wrong year for style).

provenance.abduction_hint: Date code = year authentication signal.

messages.content: A silk scarf has hand-rolled edges — small even rolls at the perimeter. What's your read?

Hand-rolled edges — quality silk scarf construction. Softer than machine-hemmed. Time-consuming to produce.

Hand-rolled edges are a labor-intensive traditional finish for silk scarves. Even, small rolls at the perimeter. Machine-hemmed is faster/cheaper but harder edge. Hand-rolled = premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-rolled scarf edge = premium construction.

messages.content: A wool sweater has visible ribbing pattern at cuffs, hem, and neckline. Call it.

Ribbed cuff/hem construction. Standard knit finish for shape retention. Higher-tier uses tighter or more elaborate ribbing.

Ribbing at cuffs, hems, and necklines helps knits retain shape. 1x1 rib (finest), 2x2 rib (standard), broader ribs (chunky). Higher-tier sweaters have tighter ribbing that recovers better after wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Ribbing = shape-retention detail.

messages.content: A knit sweater has color fields transitioning subtly across the piece. What are you looking at?

Marled yarn (dyed multi-color) or specific dyeing technique. Design-tier feature; not mass-market standard.

Marled yarns are multi-color twisted yarns. Space-dyed yarns have color transitions. Both are design details more common in higher-tier or artisan knits.

provenance.abduction_hint: Color transitions in knit = yarn design detail.

messages.content: A polo shirt has a woven collar (not knit). What's this?

Higher-tier polo shirt construction. Woven collar drapes cleaner than knit collar. Some premium brands use this construction.

Standard polo shirt has knit collar (same knit as body). Higher tier uses woven collar for cleaner drape. Slightly less flexible but more formal appearance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Woven polo collar = higher-tier construction.

messages.content: A polo shirt has 'made in Peru' with 'Pima cotton' label. What's your read?

Premium polo shirt fabric. Peruvian Pima cotton is a long-staple cotton — softer, more durable, less pilling than short-staple cotton.

Peruvian Pima cotton is a premium long-staple cotton. Longer fibers = smoother yarns, less pilling, softer hand. Egyptian, American Supima, and Peruvian Pima are the premium cotton categories.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pima cotton = premium long-staple cotton tier.

messages.content: A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Vegetable Tanned.' Call it.

Traditional tanning method. Ages beautifully to develop patina. Higher-tier leather goods construction. Premium price tier.

Vegetable tanning uses plant tannins (oak bark historically). Traditional, slow, produces leather that ages beautifully with a natural patina. Higher-tier than chrome tanning (modern, faster, most leather).

provenance.abduction_hint: Vegetable tanned = traditional, patinas beautifully.

messages.content: A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Full Grain Leather.' What are you looking at?

Highest tier of leather — natural surface, most durable. Premium leather goods marker.

Leather tiers: full-grain (natural surface, most durable, luxury), top-grain (sanded, more uniform, mid), corrected grain (heavily processed, low), bonded leather (glued pieces, lowest 'real leather'), synthetic (not real leather). Full grain is the premium marker.

provenance.abduction_hint: Full-grain leather = highest tier real leather.

messages.content: A leather bag has a stamp reading 'Genuine Leather' but the surface is uniform embossed grain. What's this?

Genuine leather is a specific low-tier designation — actually the lowest 'real leather' category. Uniform embossed grain is common.

'Genuine leather' sounds premium but is actually a specific low-tier grade — split leather or corrected-grain leather. Embossed uniform grain is standard. Priced accordingly.

provenance.abduction_hint: 'Genuine leather' label = low-tier real leather.

messages.content: A leather bag has plasticky uniform texture and cool synthetic feel. What's your read?

PU (polyurethane) or PVC synthetic — plastic, not leather. Legally not 'leather.'

Synthetic 'leathers' (PU, PVC, vegan leather) are plastic. Legally cannot be labeled 'leather' in most jurisdictions. Sometimes higher quality than corrected-grain leather in durability terms.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plasticky feel = synthetic, not leather.

messages.content: A jacket label reads 'RN 12345' inside the collar. Call it.

US textile-compliance registration number. Verifiable on US FTC database. Identifies responsible company.

RN numbers (Registered Numbers) identify the responsible company for US textile products. Lookup at ftc.gov identifies the actual maker or importer. Sometimes reveals surprising info (brand X is actually made by manufacturer Y).

provenance.abduction_hint: RN number = US regulatory ID, database lookup reveals responsible party.

messages.content: A dress has care symbols only (no written care instructions). What are you looking at?

European or international garment. Care symbols follow ISO standards. Written instructions required by US law for US-market garments.

US law requires written care instructions on labels. European garments often have only symbols. Symbol-only care may indicate non-US-market garment. Standard ISO care symbols cover wash, dry, iron, bleach, dry-clean.

provenance.abduction_hint: Symbol-only care = European/non-US market garment.

messages.content: A dress has written care instructions in multiple languages. What's this?

International or multi-market garment. Standard for larger brands with global distribution.

Multi-language care labels indicate international-market garment. Global brands standardize across markets. Not specifically a tier indicator; more of a market indicator.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-language care = global-market garment.

messages.content: A cotton shirt has 'yarn dyed' as a construction claim. What's your read?

Yarn-dyed fabric — dye applied to yarn before weaving. Deeper, more durable color. Higher-tier construction than piece-dyed.

Yarn-dyed fabric has color woven in (yarns dyed before weaving). Piece-dyed has color applied to finished fabric. Yarn-dyed has deeper, more durable color; often reveals color on the back of fabric matching the front. Higher-tier construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Yarn-dyed = premium construction, color-deep.

messages.content: A cotton shirt has faded color on the back that doesn't match the front. Call it.

Piece-dyed with washing/wear fade. Common on mass-market cotton. Higher-tier yarn-dyed doesn't fade unevenly this way.

Piece-dyed fabric can fade unevenly (light fades one way; the interior remains original color). Yarn-dyed fabric doesn't have this issue — color is uniform throughout the yarn.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uneven color front/back = piece-dyed, faded.

messages.content: A pair of jeans has selvage edge visible at rolled cuff. What are you looking at?

Selvage denim — shuttle-loom construction. Premium denim tier. Cuff often rolled to display the selvage as style feature.

Selvage denim has finished edges from shuttle-loom weaving. Often rolled at cuff to display the colored selvage line. Premium denim tier — usually $150+. Non-selvage denim has overlocked/serged edges.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible selvage line at cuff = premium denim.

messages.content: A pair of jeans has raw (dark) unwashed denim. What's this?

Raw denim. Will fade and shape to wearer over months of wear. Premium and enthusiast denim tier.

Raw denim starts dark and rigid. Fades over 6+ months of daily wear to a personalized pattern. Enthusiast tier — some collectors don't wash for a year to maximize contrast. Higher-tier construction usually.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dark rigid unwashed denim = raw denim, will personalize.

messages.content: A pair of jeans has fabric composition 'cotton with 2% elastane.' What's your read?

Stretch denim. Standard modern jean fabric. More comfortable than pure cotton but less durable long-term.

Stretch denim (with 1-3% elastane/spandex) is the modern standard. Comfort improvement over pure cotton. Elastane can fatigue over years, causing loosening or breakdown. Pure-cotton denim (100% cotton) is enthusiast/traditional tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Elastane in denim = modern stretch, comfort tier.

messages.content: A T-shirt fabric is thick and heavy compared to typical T-shirts. Call it.

Heavyweight T-shirt — 6-8oz+ fabric. Premium T-shirt construction. More durable, holds shape better.

T-shirt fabric weights: lightweight (3-5oz), midweight (5-6oz), heavyweight (6-8+oz). Heavyweight is premium — more durable, better drape, holds shape. Cost more, wear longer.

provenance.abduction_hint: Heavy T-shirt fabric = premium construction.

messages.content: A T-shirt fabric is very thin and sheer. What are you looking at?

Lightweight T-shirt fabric. Common in inexpensive or fashion-oriented tees. Less durable, less shape-holding.

Very lightweight T-shirt fabric is common in mass-market and fashion tees. Less durable, quicker to become sheer at high-wear points, less shape retention. Priced accordingly.

provenance.abduction_hint: Very thin T-shirt fabric = mass-market or fashion tier.

messages.content: A sweatshirt fabric has soft brushed interior surface. What's this?

French terry or fleece interior. Warm comfortable construction. Loopback (un-brushed) is alternative style.

Sweatshirt interior: loopback (un-brushed, textured, casual look) or fleece (brushed, soft, warm). Fleece is warmer; loopback is more breathable. Both are legitimate constructions.

provenance.abduction_hint: Brushed interior sweatshirt = fleece construction.

messages.content: A leather jacket has visible seam-tape reinforcing all interior seams. What's your read?

Higher-tier leather jacket construction. Seam-tape prevents seam stretching over years of wear.

Interior seam reinforcement is a higher-tier leather jacket construction detail. Prevents seam stretching, adds structural longevity. Common on premium leather; often absent on affordable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Seam-tape reinforcement = premium leather goods construction.

messages.content: A leather jacket has stitching that is inconsistent — some tight, some loose along a single seam. Call it.

Low-tier construction. Inconsistent stitching indicates rushed production or low quality control.

High-quality leather goods have consistent, tight, uniform stitching. Inconsistency = production quality issue. Not necessarily immediately noticeable to a buyer but degrades faster.

provenance.abduction_hint: Inconsistent stitching = lower-tier construction.

messages.content: A wool coat has an interior label reading 'MADE IN ITALY, Biella region.' What are you looking at?

Biella is a premier Italian wool-manufacturing region. Wool coat from Biella is likely premium construction.

Biella, Italy is world-renowned for wool textile manufacturing. Premium mills (Loro Piana, Cerruti, Zegna, Vitale Barberis Canonico) are based there. Biella-made wool garments are typically premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Biella origin = premium Italian wool region.

messages.content: A silk tie has 'six-fold' construction claim in the label. What's this?

Higher-quality tie construction — six folds without interlining. Between traditional 3-fold+interlining and premium 7-fold.

Tie fold counts: 3-fold with interlining (standard), 3-fold no interlining (better), 6-fold (premium), 7-fold (top). No-interlining folds require heavier silk to maintain body.

provenance.abduction_hint: 6-fold tie = premium construction tier.

messages.content: A silk tie has 'made in Como, Italy' label. What's your read?

Como, Italy is a major silk-manufacturing region. Como-made ties are typically premium.

Como, Italy has been a silk manufacturing center for centuries. Premium silk fabrics and finished silk goods often originate here. Como-made ties or silks are usually premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Como origin = premium Italian silk region.

messages.content: A wool suit has a small tag reading 'Extra Fine Merino, Grade AAA.' Call it.

High-grade merino wool. AAA grade is premium tier. Extra fine indicates fiber diameter is very fine (18-19 micron).

Merino wool grading: AAA is premium, followed by AA, A, B. Extra fine merino has fiber diameter 18-19 micron. Combined with Super numbers gives detailed fabric specification.

provenance.abduction_hint: Merino grade AAA + Extra Fine = premium wool tier.

messages.content: A dress shirt has small mother-of-pearl buttons and hand-stitched buttonholes. What are you looking at?

Premium dress shirt tier. Mother-of-pearl buttons + hand buttonholes = high-tier construction.

Combination of premium buttons + hand-worked buttonholes is a premium shirt tier signal. Typical of bespoke or high-tier ready-to-wear. Higher price point.

provenance.abduction_hint: Premium buttons + hand-worked buttonholes = premium shirt.

messages.content: A dress shirt has plastic buttons and machine buttonholes. What's this?

Standard mass-market construction. Not tier indicator alone; combined with fabric quality tells whole story.

Plastic buttons + machine buttonholes = mass-market default. Common on affordable shirts. Fabric quality, construction, brand together determine final tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plastic + machine construction = mass-market baseline.

messages.content: A trouser waistband has a curtain-lining (extra fabric strip inside the waistband). What's your read?

Curtain-lined waistband — higher-tier trouser construction. Keeps shirt tucked, provides structure.

Curtain waistband has an interior extension of lining fabric that hangs down inside the trouser. Keeps shirt tucked in, provides polish and structure. Higher-tier trouser construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Curtain waistband = higher-tier trouser construction.

messages.content: A trouser has vertical inseam that opens easily when the leg is spread. Call it.

Traditional loose-fit trouser construction. Common on classic tailored trousers.

Traditional trouser construction has a specific inseam that permits stride. Modern slim-cut trousers have tighter inseams. Loose inseam allows greater range of motion but has less-tailored appearance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Open inseam = traditional trouser construction.

messages.content: A dress has a hand-picked stitch along the zipper attachment. What are you looking at?

Traditional couture construction technique. Hand-picking prevents zipper puckering. High-end tailoring.

Hand-picking a zipper (attaching by hand with tiny stitches on the exterior side) prevents puckering and gives a couture appearance. Very time-consuming; typical of high-end dress construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-picked zipper = couture tier construction.

messages.content: A dress has invisible zipper along back seam. What's this?

Standard modern dress construction. Invisible zipper is nearly seamless; specific machine stitching required.

Invisible zippers are the modern dress standard — the zipper coil is hidden by fabric folds, appearing seamless. Requires specific machine foot for installation. Standard on modern dresses; less common on vintage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Invisible zipper = modern dress construction.

messages.content: A dress has a visible metal zipper along back seam. What's your read?

Older or utilitarian dress construction. Visible zippers were standard pre-invisible-zipper (mid-1960s development).

Visible metal zippers were standard on pre-1965 dresses. Some modern designs use visible zippers as design feature (utility, moto-jacket style). Vintage dresses typically have visible zippers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible metal zipper on dress = vintage or design choice.

messages.content: A skirt has a slit at the back seam. Call it.

Kick pleat or vent — allows walking room in fitted skirts. Standard construction detail.

Back vents and kick pleats provide walking room in fitted skirts. Standard construction; different styles have different appearances. Some slits are functional; some are style-only (higher).
A handbag closure is a spring-loaded metal turn-lock. What are you looking at?

Turn-lock closure. Traditional handbag construction. Common on structured leather bags. Higher-tier brands often use premium metal.

Turn-lock closures are traditional. Various brands use them; quality varies from cheap stamped metal to solid brass or gold-plated. Premium versions have detailed engraving.

provenance.abduction_hint: Turn-lock closure = traditional construction, quality by material.

messages.content: A handbag has a zipper closure only. What's this?

Modern casual construction. Simpler than turn-lock or flap-with-lock. Not tier-indicator alone.

Zipper-only closures are simpler and more casual. Modern crossbody bags, backpacks. Not automatically low-tier — many premium modern bags use zipper closures for functionality.

provenance.abduction_hint: Zipper-only closure = modern/casual style.

messages.content: A leather bag has an interior key-hook attached with brass hardware. What's your read?

Interior utility hardware. Key hook keeps keys attached and findable. Modern convenience feature.

Interior utility (key hooks, phone pockets, laptop sleeves) are common modern conveniences on handbags. Presence indicates thoughtful design; absence doesn't indicate lack thereof.

provenance.abduction_hint: Interior utility hardware = modern convenience feature.

messages.content: A handbag closes with a magnetic snap under the flap. Call it.

Magnetic snap closure. Convenient — one-handed opening. Common on casual bags.

Magnetic snaps are convenient one-handed openings. Common on casual crossbodies. Less secure than turn-locks (theft-prone in urban settings). Consumer preference varies.

provenance.abduction_hint: Magnetic snap = convenience over security.

messages.content: A silk scarf has hand-drawn design with brushwork visible. What are you looking at?

Painted or hand-drawn silk scarf. Higher-tier tradition (some historic French houses; some artisan makers). Priced at higher tier.

Hand-painted silk scarves are labor-intensive premium products. Brushwork visible under close inspection; slight variation from piece to piece. Traditional craft. Priced significantly above screen-printed scarves.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-brushed silk = craft-tier scarf, premium pricing.

messages.content: A silk scarf has screen-printed pattern with even color fields. What's this?

Screen-printed silk scarf. Standard construction for most branded silk scarves. Consistent colors.

Screen printing on silk produces uniform, precise pattern reproduction. Most branded silk scarves are screen-printed. Efficient, high-quality possible. Not craft-tier but excellent quality.

provenance.abduction_hint: Screen-printed silk = standard branded scarf construction.

messages.content: A shoe has a leather footbed with visible stitching around perimeter. What's your read?

Leather footbed construction. Higher-tier shoe interior. Molds to foot over time.

Leather footbeds are higher tier than fabric or foam. Mold to foot over weeks of wear. More durable, more expensive to replace. Premium shoe standard.

provenance.abduction_hint: Leather footbed = premium shoe interior.

messages.content: A shoe has a fabric-covered insole with foam padding underneath. Call it.

Standard shoe insole construction. Common on mid-tier and mass-market. Comfortable but less durable than leather.

Fabric-over-foam insoles are the standard for many affordable-to-mid shoes. Comfortable, replaceable easily. Less durable than leather; may compress over time.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fabric-over-foam insole = mid-tier standard.

messages.content: A dress shoe has a heel with visible layered leather buildup. What are you looking at?

Leather-stacked heel construction. Traditional dress shoe standard. Can be resoled/replaced.

Leather-stacked heels are traditional dress-shoe construction — layers of leather stacked and shaped. Traditional, repairable, ages nicely. Rubber heels (fully rubber block) are alternative construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Layered leather heel = traditional dress shoe.

messages.content: A dress shoe has a solid rubber heel. What's this?

Modern casual or work-shoe construction. More durable outdoors than leather heel; less traditional appearance.

Rubber heels are modern and durable. Common on work shoes, walking shoes, and modern dress shoes. Less traditional appearance than leather-stacked heel. Some hybrid heels have leather cap on rubber body.

provenance.abduction_hint: Solid rubber heel = modern/casual/work construction.

messages.content: A wool sweater has decorative hand-embroidered detail at the neckline. What's your read?

Higher-tier or artisan sweater. Hand-embroidery is time-consuming decorative detail. Priced accordingly.

Hand embroidery is a craft-tier detail. Distinct from machine embroidery (uniform, precise, faster). Adds significant value; premium tier or artisan product.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand embroidery = craft-tier detail.

messages.content: A polo shirt has an embroidered logo at the chest — thick uniform stitching. Call it.

Machine-embroidered logo. Standard branded polo construction. Neat and consistent.

Machine-embroidered logos are standard on branded polos and shirts. Precise, uniform, high-quality possible. Distinct from screen-printed logos (thinner, less durable) and woven-in logos (part of fabric).

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform embroidered logo = machine embroidery.

messages.content: A shirt cuff has a placket with a small button below the main cuff button. What are you looking at?

Gauntlet button. Traditional shirt detail — prevents forearm exposure when sleeves rolled or arms extended. Higher tier construction.

Gauntlet buttons (also called sleeve placket buttons) are small buttons on the sleeve placket. Traditional dress shirt detail; prevents the placket from gapping open. Higher-tier shirts include them; mass-market often omit.

provenance.abduction_hint: Gauntlet button = traditional dress shirt tier.

messages.content: A shirt cuff shows two buttons at the wrist — one aligned, one offset. What's this?

Adjustable cuff. Two buttons at slightly different positions allow the wearer to choose a tighter or looser fit.

Adjustable cuffs have two buttonholes and one or two buttons offset. Wearer chooses tighter or looser wrist fit. Higher-tier shirts include this; mass-market usually have single-position cuffs.

provenance.abduction_hint: Offset cuff buttons = adjustable cuff, mid-to-higher tier.

messages.content: A jacket has a small piece of contrasting fabric at the interior collar seam. What's your read?

Under-collar tape or brand-signature detail. Some brands use contrasting fabric here as signature. Some tailors use as construction detail.

Under-collar details (contrasting fabric, colored thread) are sometimes signature details on high-tier brands. Not universal; brand-specific. Verify against brand references.

provenance.abduction_hint: Under-collar detail = brand signature or construction marker.

messages.content: A dress has a bustier top with visible boning through the fabric. Call it.

Structured dress with internal boning. Formal or vintage construction. Provides shape and support.

Boning (originally whalebone; now plastic or metal) provides structure and shape in dresses. Bustier construction. Formal or corset-style dresses. Visible outline through fabric is normal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible boning = structured dress construction.

messages.content: A jacket has a working boutonnière loop under the lapel — small thread loop for stem insertion. What are you looking at?

Traditional bespoke or high-end tailoring detail. Functional flower-stem loop. Marks premium tier.

Boutonnière loops are functional loops under the lapel for holding a flower stem. Traditional detail; typical of bespoke and premium ready-to-wear. Small hand-worked loop or small metal ring.

provenance.abduction_hint: Working boutonnière loop = bespoke or premium detail.

messages.content: A jacket has a functional ticket pocket above the right hip pocket. What's this?

Traditional British sporting-jacket detail. Small ticket-holder pocket. Adds tier signal; often on higher-quality jackets.

Ticket pockets are a small pocket above the standard hip pocket, traditionally for train tickets. British tradition. Common on higher-tier tailored jackets. Adds tailoring cost.

provenance.abduction_hint: Ticket pocket = British sporting/higher-tier detail.

messages.content: A jacket has swelled edges (visible seam allowance at lapel edge). What's your read?

Swelled edge = specific tailoring detail with wider edge stitching for visual weight. Traditional British detail. Premium tailoring.

Swelled edges have wider stitching along lapels and edges (typically 1/4 inch inside the edge). Traditional detail giving visual weight. Common on higher-tier tailored jackets, especially British and Italian traditions.

provenance.abduction_hint: Swelled edge stitching = premium tailoring detail.

messages.content: A jacket interior has a small pocket labeled 'pen pocket' — narrow slot inside the breast pocket. Call it.

Traditional bespoke or premium tailoring detail. Small dedicated pen pocket. Adds utility and tier signal.

Interior utility pockets (pen pocket, glasses pocket, phone pocket) are common on higher-tier tailored jackets. Custom addition on bespoke; standard on some premium ready-to-wear brands.

provenance.abduction_hint: Interior utility pockets = premium tailoring detail.

messages.content: A jacket has a specific number of interior pockets — six visible. What are you looking at?

Feature-rich construction. More than the standard 2-3 interior pockets. Suggests bespoke or premium construction.

Standard jacket has 2-3 interior pockets. 4-6 pockets suggests custom or premium construction (bespoke wants pockets for wallet, phone, glasses, tickets, business cards, etc.). Higher tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multiple interior pockets = bespoke/premium tier.

messages.content: A trouser hem shows French tack (small chain-stitched thread between lining and trouser hem). What's this?

Higher-tier tailoring detail. French tack allows lining to move independently of trouser, preventing pulls.

French tack (also called swing tack) is a small chain-stitched thread connecting lining to trouser hem. Allows lining independent movement, preventing pulls. High-quality tailoring detail. Rare on affordable trousers.

provenance.abduction_hint: French tack = high-quality tailoring detail.

messages.content: A tuxedo has satin-covered buttons on the front. What's your read?

Traditional tuxedo construction. Satin covers the buttons to prevent visual disruption. Formal detail.

Tuxedo buttons are traditionally satin-covered (same fabric as lapel facing). Preserves the formal aesthetic. Modern tuxedos may deviate. Traditional construction is more formal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Satin-covered buttons on tuxedo = traditional formal.

messages.content: A jacket has a soft, drooping shoulder line with visible sleeve-head puckering (con rollino). Call it.

Neapolitan tailoring signature — 'spalla camicia' (shirt shoulder) with 'con rollino' puckering. Traditional detail.

Neapolitan spalla camicia has a soft shoulder with the sleeve head slightly higher than shoulder. Con rollino is intentional puckering at the sleeve seam. Signature of Neapolitan tailoring tradition.

provenance.abduction_hint: Puckered sleeve head + soft shoulder = Neapolitan tailoring.

messages.content: A jacket has a very slim silhouette with three-button-two-roll closure (lapel rolls to hide top button). What are you looking at?

Modern tailoring style — three-roll-two. Contemporary aesthetic; higher-tier or fashion-oriented.

Three-roll-two = three buttons on jacket, but lapel rolls to hide the top button (only middle button used). Modern aesthetic; less formal than two-button, more contemporary than three-button. Premium/fashion tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Three-roll-two = modern tailoring style.

messages.content: A tie shows a subtle geometric pattern with 6-8 colors in the weave, no printing. What's this?

Woven tie (jacquard). Higher-tier construction than printed. Colors are in the yarn, not on the surface. Very durable.

Jacquard-woven ties have the pattern in the weave (multiple colors of yarn interlaced). Higher-tier than printed ties. Colors are integral to the fabric, not on the surface. Very durable and premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Woven pattern with multi-color yarns = jacquard, premium tie tier.

messages.content: A tie shows a pattern with color solidly on the surface but visible weave-white on the back. What's your read?

Printed tie. Color applied to surface only; back shows the base fabric color. Lower-tier than woven.

Printed ties have the design applied to the surface (usually silk twill), leaving the back showing base fabric color. Lower-tier than jacquard-woven ties. Still can be premium if the silk is high-quality.

provenance.abduction_hint: Surface-only color on tie = printed construction.

messages.content: A dress has boning that follows the natural body curves — subtle, invisible from outside. Call it.

Custom/bespoke or couture dress. Precise boning placement invisible externally is a high-end detail.

Boning that follows body curves invisibly is a couture-level construction. Contrast with visible or crude boning on lesser dresses. Very high-end construction; either bespoke or top-tier ready-to-wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Invisible precise boning = couture-tier dress.

messages.content: A dress has multiple layers of underlining, canvas, and finishing at the bodice. What are you looking at?

Structured couture dress construction. Multiple internal layers provide shape and support without external boning.

High-end dress construction can include multiple internal layers (interlining, underlining, canvas, boning, finishing) for shape. Couture-tier dresses have detailed internal architecture. Craft-tier signal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multiple internal layers = couture-tier construction.

messages.content: A silk dress has a French seam finish throughout (all seams fully enclosed in fabric tubes). What's this?

Very high-end dress construction. French seams throughout is labor-intensive; typical of couture or high-end ready-to-wear.

French seams enclose all raw edges within a fabric tube. On lightweight fabrics (silk, chiffon), this is the highest-quality construction. Very time-consuming; typical only of couture or premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: French seams throughout = couture construction.

messages.content: A wool coat has hand-basted stitching visible along the interior canvas. What's your read?

Bespoke or premium ready-to-wear construction. Hand-basting the canvas is a marker of high-end tailoring.

Interior canvas hand-basting (small, slightly-visible thread securing the canvas to fabric) is a bespoke or high-end tailoring marker. Fully-machined canvas exists but hand-basting is craft-tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-basted canvas visible = bespoke or premium tailoring.

messages.content: A jacket has a Milanese buttonhole on the lapel — rolled, dense, precise. Call it.

High-end bespoke or ready-to-wear detail. Milanese buttonhole (hand-worked, rolled edge, precise) is a craft-tier marker.

Milanese buttonhole is a specific traditional hand-worked buttonhole style. Rolled edge, dense stitching, keyhole shape. Hand-worked is craft-tier; machine-done Milanese-style exists but is a mimicry.

provenance.abduction_hint: True Milanese buttonhole = hand-worked, craft-tier.

messages.content: A jacket has 'canvas visible under lapel' — buyer can lift lapel and see internal canvas. What are you looking at?

Full-canvas construction confirmable by lifting lapel. Premium tier marker.

Full-canvas construction can be verified by feeling and lifting the lapel — canvas is visible/felt as separate from the fabric. Fused construction won't show canvas because it's glued to the fabric. Definitive test.

provenance.abduction_hint: Canvas visible under lapel = confirmed full-canvas construction.

messages.content: A dress shirt has a two-piece back yoke. What's this?

Higher-tier shirt construction. Two-piece back yoke has a seam in the middle, allowing pattern matching or fit shaping. Premium tier.

Back yoke construction: one-piece (mass-market) vs two-piece (higher tier). Two-piece allows better fit shaping over shoulders and pattern-matched checks/stripes. Premium shirt tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-piece back yoke = premium shirt tier.

messages.content: A dress shirt has pattern matching at the pocket — pocket pattern aligns exactly with the body pattern. What's your read?

Higher-tier shirt construction. Pattern matching at pockets is labor-intensive; requires larger fabric use. Premium tier.

Pattern matching (aligning stripes, checks, plaids exactly across pocket seams) is a labor-intensive detail. Requires more fabric and precision. Premium and bespoke tier. Mass-market shirts don't pattern-match.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pattern matching = premium construction detail.

messages.content: A jacket has hand-stitched lapel roll — the lapel folds precisely with subtle roll from hand shaping. Call it.

Bespoke or premium tier — hand-shaped lapel roll is a craft detail. Precise, subtle, well-executed.

Hand-shaped lapel roll (via hand-pressing and internal canvas work) creates a specific 3D lapel curve. Distinguishes bespoke from mass-produced. Feel and appearance both signal craft-tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-shaped lapel roll = bespoke/premium craft.

messages.content: A cashmere sweater label reads 'inner Mongolia' as origin. What are you looking at?

Prime cashmere origin region. Inner Mongolia produces premium cashmere fiber. Well-executed sweaters from this region are top tier.

Inner Mongolia is a primary cashmere production region — the climate produces very fine goat undercoat. Not all Inner Mongolia cashmere is premium; construction quality matters. But origin is a positive signal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Inner Mongolia = premium cashmere region.

messages.content: A cashmere sweater has fiber diameter noted as '15 micron.' What's this?

Very fine cashmere — 15 micron is exceptional. Baby cashmere or high-grade tier. Premium market.

Cashmere fiber diameter: standard 16-19 micron. Baby cashmere 13-15 micron. 15 micron is very fine, close to baby cashmere. Extraordinary softness. Premium price.

provenance.abduction_hint: 15 micron cashmere = ultra-premium tier.

messages.content: A cashmere sweater label reads 'baby cashmere.' What's your read?

Ultra-premium tier. Baby cashmere is from goat kids' first shearing — finest and rarest cashmere.

Baby cashmere comes from the first shearing of goat kids (before adult coat develops). Very fine fiber (13-15 micron), extremely soft. Extremely rare. Priced accordingly — several times standard cashmere.

provenance.abduction_hint: Baby cashmere = ultra-premium rare tier.

messages.content: A silk garment has a subtle sheen that changes as the fabric moves. Call it.

Consistent with mulberry silk. Mulberry silk has a characteristic changing sheen. Premium silk tier.

Mulberry silk (from silkworms fed mulberry leaves) is the premium silk. Fine, smooth, characteristic changing sheen. Tussah silk (wild silk) is coarser, less sheen, less uniform.

provenance.abduction_hint: Changing sheen on silk = mulberry silk (premium).

messages.content: A silk garment has visible slubs (irregular thick spots) in the weave. What are you looking at?

Dupioni silk or tussah silk. Slubs are characteristic. Different silk category from mulberry. Rougher texture, distinct look.

Dupioni silk has visible slubs from irregular yarn. Distinct textured look. Not lower-quality automatically; different aesthetic. Popular in decor and fashion contexts wanting texture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Slubs in silk = dupioni or tussah, textural silk category.

messages.content: A wool blazer feels heavier than expected for its size. What's this?

Substantial wool fabric — heavier fabric is often (not always) higher tier. Could also indicate synthetic-blend or heavily-lined construction.

Wool fabric weight varies by intended use. Winter wool coats and heavier blazers are naturally heavy. Extra weight can also come from synthetic blends (polyester adds weight without warmth) or heavy lining. Not weight-alone as tier indicator.

provenance.abduction_hint: Heavy blazer = investigate: could be premium heavy wool or synthetic blend.

messages.content: A wool blazer feels very lightweight compared to typical wool blazers. What's your read?

Lightweight wool — Super 130s or higher. Suitable for warmer climates or transitional seasons. Premium tier.

Lightweight wool (Super 130s+) is used for warm-climate wearing or transitional seasons. Delicate, luxurious hand. Premium tier construction. Not for cold weather; not everyday-wear durability.

provenance.abduction_hint: Lightweight wool feel = high super number, warm-weather tier.

messages.content: A jacket has 'MTM' or 'made to measure' label. Call it.

Made-to-measure garment — customized to specific measurements from an existing pattern. Between ready-to-wear and bespoke tiers.

MTM (made-to-measure) uses existing patterns adjusted to individual measurements. Higher tier than ready-to-wear; lower tier than bespoke (which is fully custom pattern). Common at premium tailoring houses.

provenance.abduction_hint: MTM label = customized tier, between RTW and bespoke.

messages.content: A jacket has 'bespoke' claim in interior label. What are you looking at?

Fully custom construction from custom pattern. Highest tier. Verify by examining hand-work indicators.

Bespoke = fully custom pattern + fitting + hand construction. Highest tailoring tier. Some brands misuse the term for MTM. Verify by hand-work markers (pick stitching, hand buttonholes, canvas).

provenance.abduction_hint: Bespoke claim = highest tier, verify with construction detail check.

messages.content: A shirt cuff has round French-cuff construction — folded double-cuff with cuff-link holes. What's this?

French cuff shirt — formal tier. Requires cuff-links to wear. Higher formality than barrel cuff.

French cuff (double cuff) is folded and secured with cuff-links. Formal tier. Traditional business/formal shirt style. Contemporary casual shirts don't use French cuffs.

provenance.abduction_hint: French cuff = formal shirt tier.

messages.content: A shirt cuff has convertible construction — can be worn as barrel or French cuff. What's your read?

Convertible cuff. Versatile — barrel or French cuff. Compromise style; less formal than dedicated French, more formal than barrel.

Convertible cuffs have buttonholes for both button and cuff-link options. Versatile — casual button use or formal cuff-link use. Compromise construction; less traditional than dedicated French cuff.

provenance.abduction_hint: Convertible cuff = versatile mid-formal tier.

messages.content: A silk tie has an interlining that is very thick and firm. Call it.

Traditional wool or silk-blend interlining. Contributes to knot shape and drape. Higher tier tie.

Traditional tie interlining is wool or wool-blend fabric. Provides body and shape retention. Firmer interlining = firmer knot, better drape. Higher-tier ties have thicker interlining.

provenance.abduction_hint: Thick firm tie interlining = premium tier.

messages.content: A silk tie has a thin, flexible interlining. What are you looking at?

Standard mass-market tie construction. Modern preference for softer, flexible drape.

Modern thin interlining creates a more flexible drape. Common on affordable-to-mid ties. Some premium designers prefer this style. Not a bad construction; different aesthetic.

provenance.abduction_hint: Thin flexible tie interlining = modern mass-market or aesthetic choice.

messages.content: A silk tie has no interlining. What's this?

Unlined tie. Very soft drape; unique aesthetic. Some premium designers prefer this; others consider it lower-tier.

Unlined ties have no interlining. Very soft drape, floppy knot. Aesthetic choice. Some premium designers use unlined for a specific look; some low-end ties are unlined for cost. Context-dependent.

provenance.abduction_hint: Unlined tie = aesthetic choice or cost-saving.

messages.content: A wool sweater has moth damage — small hole near shoulder seam. What's your read?

Damage. Small hole reparable with darning or invisible mending. Discount modestly; more if multiple holes.

Single moth hole is typically reparable ($30-100 professional invisible mending). Multiple holes suggest active moth infestation OR long-past infestation; verify. Cedar and lavender protect against moths.

provenance.abduction_hint: Single moth hole = reparable, modest discount.

messages.content: A leather bag has visible marks from a magnet clasp indicating repeated contact wear. Call it.

Wear pattern from clasp. Not surprising on leather bags with magnetic closures. Some patina; assess overall condition.

Magnetic clasps eventually wear leather at contact points. Small dark marks develop. Not damage per se — patina. Context-dependent whether desirable or issue.

provenance.abduction_hint: Clasp-contact wear on leather = expected patina.

messages.content: A dress shirt has all details premium (buttons, buttonholes, construction) but the fabric is thin and cheap. What are you looking at?

Inconsistent construction — premium details on cheap fabric. Possibly refurbished by adding better details; possibly manufacturer inconsistency.

Inconsistency in construction level (premium details on cheap fabric) is unusual. Could indicate the shirt was refurbished (upgraded details on cheap base) or the manufacturer inconsistent. Ask before assuming.

provenance.abduction_hint: Construction inconsistency = investigation warranted.

messages.content: A jacket has a bespoke-quality construction throughout but the fit is clearly wrong for typical body types. What's this?

Bespoke jacket made for a specific individual. Fit reflects that person's measurements. Alterations may improve fit but limits how much.

Bespoke garments are made for specific individuals. Fit-testing before buying is important — bespoke doesn't necessarily fit anyone else well. Some alterations possible but limited. Buy only if fits well or close-to-fits.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bespoke jacket + odd fit = made for someone else, fit test critical.

messages.content: A trouser has 'grosgrain waistband' construction. What's your read?

Grosgrain fabric on interior waistband — provides structure and prevents shirt slippage. Premium trouser tier.

Grosgrain (heavy ribbed silk or synthetic) waistband material provides structure and grip. Higher tier than plain fabric waistband. Common on higher-tier trousers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Grosgrain waistband = premium trouser tier.

messages.content: A trouser has 'no split rear seam' (single-piece rear panel). Call it.

Requires more fabric — higher tier construction. Also allows for different fits.

Rear seam construction: single-piece panel (higher tier, no back seam) vs two-piece with center-back seam (standard). Single-piece requires more fabric; cleaner appearance. Premium construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Single-piece rear = premium fabric use.

messages.content: A shirt has removable collar stays made of brass. What are you looking at?

Higher tier — brass stays are more durable than plastic. Some very-high-end shirts use mother-of-pearl stays.

Collar stay materials: plastic (standard), brass or metal (higher tier — more durable, more expensive), mother-of-pearl or bone (very-high-end, rare).

provenance.abduction_hint: Brass collar stays = higher-tier shirt.

messages.content: A shirt has stitched-in collar stays that can't be removed. What's this?

Fixed collar stays. Alternative construction. Some higher-tier shirts use this to prevent stay loss. Others use fixed as cost-saving.

Fixed collar stays are either cost-saving (mass-market don't want stays to be lost) or premium anti-loss (fixed stays never lost, cleaner collar line). Context determines which.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fixed collar stays = cost-saving or anti-loss premium choice.

messages.content: A shirt has a placket construction where the underside placket is folded into 6+ layers of fabric. What's your read?

Higher-tier construction. Multi-layer underside placket adds durability at high-stress button area.

Multi-layer underside placket construction adds body and durability where buttons attach. Higher tier construction. Simple two-layer underside is standard mass-market.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-layer placket = premium construction.

messages.content: A jacket has 'quarter-lined' construction — lining only in shoulders and upper back. Call it.

Very casual or warm-climate construction. Interior structure visible. Not tier signal in either direction.

Quarter-lining is minimal — lining only in shoulders and upper back. Common on casual jackets, warm-climate garments, deconstructed jackets. Aesthetic and functional choice; not tier signal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Quarter-lining = casual/warm-climate construction.

messages.content: A knit garment has 'fully-fashioned' construction — the pieces are knit to shape, not cut from knit fabric. What are you looking at?

Higher tier knitwear. Fully-fashioned means the sweater pieces are knit in their final shape (with shaping stitches at seams). No cutting waste; superior finish.

Fully-fashioned knits are shaped as they're knit (versus cut-and-sewn where knit fabric is cut and sewn like woven fabric). Superior finish, no fraying, better fit. Premium tier construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fully-fashioned = premium knitwear construction.

messages.content: A knit sweater shows cut-and-sew construction — knit fabric edges visible at seams. What's this?

Standard mass-market knit construction. Fabric knit then cut and sewn. Standard for T-shirts and affordable knit garments.

Cut-and-sew knits use pre-knit fabric cut into pieces. Standard for T-shirts, casual sweatshirts, mass-market knits. Faster and cheaper than fully-fashioned. Standard tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cut-and-sew = mass-market knit construction.

messages.content: A dress has multiple layers — inner slip, base fabric, decorative overlay. What's your read?

Complex layered dress construction. Premium tier or couture. Higher retail price.

Layered dress construction (slip + base + overlay + trim) is time-consuming. Common in premium tier and couture. Higher retail price. Verify all layers are in good condition.

provenance.abduction_hint: Multi-layer dress = premium/couture construction.

messages.content: A wool coat has 'melton' fabric — dense, felted, water-resistant. Call it.

Traditional coat fabric — melton is a heavy felted wool. Water-resistant, warm. Naval and military traditional; still used in premium coats.

Melton is a dense felted wool fabric. Water-resistant, warm, traditional. Peacoats and naval coats often melton. Higher-tier winter coats use melton. Very durable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Melton fabric = traditional heavy winter wool.

messages.content: A wool coat has 'boiled wool' fabric — thick, felted, unique texture. What are you looking at?

European tradition (Austrian, German, Scandinavian). Warm, distinctive texture. Common in loden-tradition coats.

Boiled wool is felted (shrunk and matted intentionally) creating dense, distinctive texture. Warm, water-resistant, traditional. Loden coats (Alpine tradition), some cardigans and jackets. Distinct aesthetic.

provenance.abduction_hint: Boiled wool = Alpine tradition, distinctive texture.

messages.content: A camel-hair coat has visible fine cream-colored hair fibers throughout. What's this?

Genuine camel-hair coat. Warm, luxurious, distinctive color. High tier winter coat.

Camel hair (from Bactrian camel undercoat) is warm and luxurious. Distinctive cream/tan color. High tier winter coat fabric. Some 'camel-hair' is blended; check label for percentage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cream color + fine visible hair = camel hair, premium tier.

messages.content: A silk garment has 'charmeuse' construction — one side matte, one side shiny. What's your read?

Charmeuse silk — satin-weave with one glossy face and one matte face. Common in lingerie, dresses, blouses. Premium silk category.

Charmeuse silk has satin weave — glossy on one side, matte on reverse. Common in evening wear and lingerie. Fluid drape, slippery hand. Premium silk category.

provenance.abduction_hint: Two-face silk (glossy/matte) = charmeuse.

messages.content: A silk garment has 'crepe' texture — slightly pebbled surface. Call it.

Crepe silk — silk with twisted yarns producing pebbled texture. Elegant drape; common in dresses. Premium silk category.

Crepe silk has slight pebbled or grainy surface from twisted yarns. Elegant drape, less shiny than satin/charmeuse. Common in dresses and blouses. Premium silk category.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pebbled silk texture = crepe construction.

messages.content: A cotton garment has visible slub yarns randomly through the fabric. What are you looking at?

Slub cotton — decorative yarn variation. Common in bohemian and premium casual garments. Style choice.

Slub cotton yarns have intentional thick/thin variations. Bohemian and premium casual style. Not defect. Adds visual texture and character to plain fabrics.

provenance.abduction_hint: Slub cotton = intentional textural style.

messages.content: A wool coat label reads '100% cashmere' with high tag price and visible chemical smell. What's this?

Suspicious — chemical smell could indicate treatment residue OR could indicate the garment isn't real cashmere. Investigate.

Chemical smells on garments can indicate: dye residue (normal for new garments), formaldehyde treatment (some wool processing), or fake fiber. Real cashmere doesn't have strong chemical smell. Investigate.

provenance.abduction_hint: Strong chemical smell + luxury claim = investigate for authenticity.

messages.content: A dress shirt has functional interior placket with a small metal support to keep the placket flat. What's your read?

High-tier detail. Interior metal support prevents placket puckering. Craft-tier construction.

Interior placket supports (small metal strips or firm interlining) prevent placket puckering and maintain crisp appearance. Craft-tier construction; common on bespoke shirts.

provenance.abduction_hint: Interior placket support = craft-tier construction.

messages.content: A cashmere sweater is unusually stretchy for cashmere. Call it.

Suspicious — pure cashmere has limited stretch. May be a wool-cashmere blend or synthetic-blend not disclosed. Investigate.

Pure cashmere has limited stretch. Excessive stretch suggests blend with wool (limited stretch increase) or synthetic (major stretch increase). Verify label; consider fabric burn test if legally possible.

provenance.abduction_hint: Excessive stretch in claimed cashmere = investigate composition.

messages.content: A jacket lining has a subtle brand pattern woven in that matches a specific known brand's reference. What are you looking at?

Authentic-consistent — verifiable brand identifier. Reference-check for exact match on pattern scale, colors, weave.

Brand-signature linings (specific patterns, colors, weaves) are authentication signals. Verify exactly against reference photos — pattern scale, color, thread count, weave direction all matter. Off by even small amounts = counterfeit.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pattern-matched brand lining = authentic-consistent.

messages.content: A leather bag has stitching on the strap that's a slightly different color than the body of the bag. What's this?

Standard leather goods construction. Straps often have different color stitching (for durability visibility). Not authenticity issue.

Different stitching colors on straps vs body are standard leather goods construction. Strap stitching is often more visible (heavier thread for strength). Not authenticity issue. Compare to brand standards.

provenance.abduction_hint: Different strap stitching color = standard construction.

messages.content: A dress has hem finished with hand-worked bias binding. What's your read?

Very high-tier construction. Hand-bound hem is couture-level construction. Priced accordingly.

Hem-binding by hand is a very time-consuming couture-level construction. Bias binding hides the raw edge with a strip of fabric folded and hand-stitched over it. Couture-tier detail.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-bound hem = couture-tier construction.

messages.content: A jacket has 'hand-tacked lining' — lining attached at multiple points to prevent shifting. Call it.

Premium construction. Hand-tacked lining prevents shifting inside the jacket over time. Common on premium bespoke or ready-to-wear.

Hand-tacked lining uses small hand-worked stitches at multiple points to attach lining. Prevents shifting and lengthens jacket life. Premium bespoke and ready-to-wear construction. Common on higher-tier pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-tacked lining = premium construction.

messages.content: A white plate is thin and shows shadow when held to a bright light. What are you looking at?

Porcelain or bone china — translucent body.

Translucency is the primary distinguishing feature of porcelain and bone china from other ceramic bodies. Hold to bright light; if you can see silhouettes through the piece, it's translucent.

provenance.abduction_hint: Translucency = porcelain/bone china family.

messages.content: A white plate is thick and opaque, doesn't transmit any light. What's this?

Earthenware, stoneware, or opaque porcelain (rare). Not translucent.

Opaque bodies are earthenware, stoneware, or opaque porcelain (which is unusual). Most white opaque plates are stoneware or ironstone (a specific hard earthenware tradition).

provenance.abduction_hint: Opaque + white = stoneware/ironstone/earthenware, not fine porcelain.

messages.content: A cup is very lightweight for its size and rings clearly when tapped. What's your read?

Porcelain or bone china. Vitreous body rings; light weight suggests thin fine wall.

Fine porcelain and bone china are both light and ring musically. Two-second musical ring vs one-second thud distinguishes fine ware from earthenware.

provenance.abduction_hint: Light + rings = fine porcelain/bone china.

messages.content: A cup is heavy for its size and thuds when tapped. Call it.

Earthenware or stoneware, or possibly a cracked/damaged vitreous piece.

Heavy + thud = usually earthenware. Confirmed if opaque and thick. Damaged/cracked pieces of any body may thud due to the crack.

provenance.abduction_hint: Heavy + thud = earthenware or damaged piece.

messages.content: The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is white. What are you looking at?

White-bodied ceramic — porcelain, bone china, or ironstone. Foot color indicates body color.

The foot ring shows body color because glaze is scraped from where the piece sits in the kiln. White body could be porcelain, bone china, or white-bodied earthenware/ironstone.

provenance.abduction_hint: White foot = white body family.

messages.content: The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is cream-colored. What's this?

Creamware or later cream-bodied earthenware. Common British tradition.

Cream body under a white glaze is creamware. Wedgwood pioneered creamware in the 1760s. Continued in various forms through the 20th century.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cream foot = creamware tradition.

messages.content: The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is gray. What's your read?

Stoneware — non-porous vitreous body. Higher fire than earthenware.

Gray body is characteristic of stoneware. Common in utility ware, salt-glaze pieces, and modern studio pottery. Non-porous, food-safe.

provenance.abduction_hint: Gray foot = stoneware body.

messages.content: The bottom of a plate shows an unglazed foot ring where the body is red-orange. Call it.

Redware — early American or folk-tradition earthenware.

Redware is a classic folk-pottery tradition. Early American and European folk pottery, generally utility ware. Not the tier of porcelain but historically significant.

provenance.abduction_hint: Red/orange foot = redware, folk tradition.

messages.content: The bottom of a piece has a mark 'MADE IN JAPAN' with a maker symbol. What are you looking at?

Post-1921 Japanese export. 'Japan' mark required after 1921 for US import.

US McKinley Tariff Act 1891 required country of origin on imports. Japan initially marked 'Nippon' until 1921, then 'Japan.' Post-WWII (1945-1952) marked 'Made in Occupied Japan.'

provenance.abduction_hint: 'Japan' mark = 1921+ Japanese export.

messages.content: The bottom of a piece has a mark 'NIPPON' only. What's this?

Pre-1921 Japanese export. 'Nippon' was the required mark 1891-1921.

'Nippon' marks date pieces to 1891-1921. Collectible category — 'Nippon porcelain' has an active collector market for fine hand-painted pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: 'Nippon' mark = 1891-1921 Japanese export.

messages.content: The bottom of a piece has 'MADE IN OCCUPIED JAPAN.' What's your read?

1945-1952 Japanese export. Specific post-WWII marking. Collector category.

'Made in Occupied Japan' was required 1945-1952 during US occupation. Distinct collector market for this narrow window of production.

provenance.abduction_hint: 'Occupied Japan' = 1945-1952 window.

messages.content: A cup and saucer set has a printed pattern that looks identical on all pieces. Call it.

Transfer print — engraved plate to paper to ceramic. Mechanical reproduction. 1750s+ tradition.

Transfer print revolutionized ceramic decoration. Copper plate engraved with design, printed to tissue paper, applied to ceramic, fired. Uniform pattern reproduction across pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform pattern across pieces = transfer print.

messages.content: A plate has a pattern with visible small brush marks and slight color variation. What are you looking at?

Hand-painted decoration — higher tier than transfer or decal. Each piece slightly unique.

Hand-painted pieces have visible brush strokes and small variations. Higher decorative tier than mechanical methods. May be signed by decorator.

provenance.abduction_hint: Brush variation = hand-painted, premium tier.

messages.content: A plate has a decorative pattern with visible edge where the pattern ends abruptly. What's this?

Decal — printed sticker applied and fired. Mass-market decoration technique. Edge visible under close inspection.

Decals are printed sheets applied to the ceramic surface then fired. Uniform pattern reproduction; visible edge distinguishes from transfer print (which has no distinct edge).

provenance.abduction_hint: Visible pattern edge = decal decoration.

messages.content: A teapot has raised gold decoration that shows wear at the handle and spout. What's your read?

Overglaze gilding. Wears with washing and use — common wear pattern on gold-decorated ceramics.

Overglaze gold is fired at lower temperature than the base glaze. Sits on top and wears away over time and washing. Don't dishwash gold-decorated pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Worn gold = overglaze technique, expected wear.

messages.content: A blue-and-white plate has cobalt-blue decoration in a chinoiserie pattern. Call it.

Blue and white porcelain — could be Chinese export, Dutch Delft, English willow-pattern, or many other traditions. Reference specific pattern to identify.
