provenance.abduction_hint: Diamond blade = hard-material cutting, verify configuration.

messages.content: A chisel has 'IYAROI' or Japanese-style laminated bevel visible. What's this?

Japanese chisel with laminated blade. Hard steel bonded to softer iron. Different sharpening technique than Western chisels.

Japanese chisels have laminated blades (hard steel welded to softer iron for structure). Very sharp cutting edge; requires specific sharpening technique. Higher tier tool tradition.

provenance.abduction_hint: Japanese laminated chisel = premium Eastern tradition.

messages.content: A large machinist's square has 'STARRETT NO. 55' or specific numeric model. What's your read?

Starrett No. 55 combination square. High precision. Reference lookup for specific era and configuration.

Starrett combination squares are premium machinist tools. Various models (No. 33, No. 55, others) for different applications. Complete sets with center-finder and protractor heads command premium.

provenance.abduction_hint: Starrett No. 55 = specific premium tool.

messages.content: A framing hammer has 'HART' brand and titanium head. Call it.

Hart framing hammer with titanium head. Premium tier. Weight reduction without energy loss.

Hart makes premium framing hammers, including titanium-headed models. High tier for professional framing carpentry. Reduce fatigue on long day-work.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hart titanium hammer = premium framing tool.

messages.content: A tape measure has 'FASTCAP' brand or specific magnetic-tip feature. What are you looking at?

FastCap or similar premium tape measure. Magnetic tip is useful feature. Various premium features.

Premium tape measures have features like magnetic tips, easily-readable printing, wider blade, standout distance (blade extends unsupported). Milwaukee, FastCap, others premium tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Premium tape measure = specific features.

messages.content: A cordless drill has integrated LED work light. What's this?

Modern drill feature. Illuminates work area. Standard on many post-2015 cordless drills.

LED work lights on cordless drills illuminate work area. Standard on modern drills (post-2015 mostly). Helpful for dim areas. Some LED brightness varies by model.

provenance.abduction_hint: LED work light = modern drill feature.

messages.content: A cordless drill has hammer-drill capability but is much smaller than a rotary hammer. What's your read?

Combi-drill hammer function. Suitable for occasional masonry drilling. Rotary hammers are heavier duty.

Combi-drill hammer function is for occasional masonry work. Rotary hammers are dedicated masonry-drilling tools with much more power. Match tool to application.

provenance.abduction_hint: Combi hammer = occasional masonry, not heavy-duty.

messages.content: A rotary hammer has 'BOSCH SDS-PLUS' or specific SDS system. Call it.

SDS-Plus rotary hammer. SDS (Slotted Drive System) chuck for masonry bits. Various SDS variants (SDS-Plus, SDS-Max) for different bit sizes.

SDS chuck systems: SDS-Plus (common, medium sizes), SDS-Max (larger bits, more power), SDS-Top (rare). Bits are not cross-compatible between systems.

provenance.abduction_hint: SDS system = specific masonry-bit compatibility.

messages.content: A wood chisel has 'BLUE SPRUCE TOOLWORKS' or similar boutique maker signature. What are you looking at?

Boutique premium chisel maker. Very high tier. High resale.

Boutique chisel makers (Blue Spruce, Sterling, Barr, others) command premium prices in woodworking market. Very high tier. Individually made or small-batch. Very high resale.

provenance.abduction_hint: Boutique chisel = premium hand-made tier.

messages.content: A hand saw has 'BAD AXE' or 'WEN-ZLOFF' brand — modern boutique maker. What's this?

Boutique hand saw. Very high tier modern hand saw. Premium resale.

Boutique hand saw makers (Bad Axe Tool Works, Wenzloff and Sons, others) make premium hand saws in the tradition of vintage saws. Very high tier. High resale.

provenance.abduction_hint: Boutique hand saw = premium modern.

messages.content: A saw sharpening service has been done recently — teeth are sharp and set. What's your read?

Recently sharpened hand saw. Ready to use. Sharp teeth, proper set. Premium user's or reseller's condition.

Hand saw sharpening includes filing teeth to proper angle and setting alternating teeth left/right. Recently sharpened saw is ready for use. Sharpening services $15-30 for hand saws.

provenance.abduction_hint: Sharpened hand saw = ready to use.

messages.content: A miter saw has laser guide and shadow-cast dust-line feature. Call it.

Modern miter saw with advanced features. Prosumer/pro tier. Shadow-cast dust line is a specific Kapex-style feature.

Shadow-cast dust lines (blade-shadow visible line) are more accurate than laser guides for cutting to line. Introduced by Festool Kapex; other brands added. Premium feature.

provenance.abduction_hint: Shadow dust line = premium miter saw feature.

messages.content: A power tool has 'M12' Milwaukee 12V compact platform mark. What are you looking at?

Milwaukee M12 platform. Compact 12V. Multiple tools in platform. Batteries cross-compatible within M12.

Milwaukee M12 = compact 12V platform. Various tools: drill, impact driver, sub-compact circular saw, band saw, right-angle drill, etc. Batteries share within platform. Distinct from M18.

provenance.abduction_hint: M12 = compact 12V Milwaukee platform.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a 'JOINTER' with 8-inch cutter head. What's this?

8-inch jointer for edge and face preparation of lumber. Serious woodworking tool. Verify motor and cutter head condition.

Jointers prepare edges and faces of lumber for gluing. 6-inch = homeshop; 8-inch = serious woodworking; 12-inch+ = professional. Cutter head condition (blade sharpness) matters.

provenance.abduction_hint: 8-inch jointer = serious woodworking tool.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a 'PLANER' or 'THICKNESSER' with 20-inch capacity. What's your read?

Large-capacity thickness planer. Handles wide boards. Professional or serious hobbyist tool. Motor and cutter head are key.

Thickness planers reduce lumber to uniform thickness. Consumer sizes 12-13-inch; larger 15-20+ inch professional. Cutter head (straight blades vs helical) affects finish and blade maintenance.

provenance.abduction_hint: Large planer = professional tier woodworking tool.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a 'BANDSAW' with 17-inch or larger throat capacity. Call it.

Larger bandsaw. Handles thick resawing and larger workpieces. Prosumer or professional.

Bandsaw throat capacity (distance from blade to column) determines maximum resaw width. 14-inch entry; 17-18-inch prosumer; 20+ professional. Larger requires more space and higher motor rating.

provenance.abduction_hint: Large bandsaw = prosumer/pro tier.

messages.content: A vintage wood-working plane has 'RECORD' English mark. What are you looking at?

Record — English plane maker, 20th century (also chisels, vises). Various tiers. Older Record often preferred to newer.

Record (English) made hand planes and other woodworking tools. Older Record (pre-1990s) often preferred to newer production. Various models parallel to Stanley.

provenance.abduction_hint: Record English plane = era matters for value.

messages.content: A hand tool marked 'PILING WEDGE' or specific specialized use. What's this?

Specialty tool. Value depends on collector market for that specific tool type. Reference lookup for niche tools.

Specialty tools (piling wedges, spokeshaves, specialized planes, uncommon chisels) have narrow collector markets. Some highly valued by specific specialties. Reference lookup for niche tools.

provenance.abduction_hint: Specialty tool = niche collector market.

messages.content: A power tool platform is 'RYOBI' 18V+ or 'KOBALT' with battery. What's your read?

Consumer tier cordless platforms. Adequate for occasional homeowner use. Lower tier than professional brands.

Ryobi and Kobalt are consumer-tier cordless platforms. Adequate for occasional use. Lower tier resale than professional (Milwaukee, DeWalt, Makita). Ryobi has wide tool selection.

provenance.abduction_hint: Ryobi/Kobalt = consumer tier.

messages.content: A jigsaw has 'BOSCH BARREL-GRIP' handle design. Call it.

Bosch premium jigsaw. Barrel-grip design. Precise cutting. High tier.

Bosch jigsaws come in barrel-grip and top-handle designs. Barrel-grip preferred for detail work. Premium models offer variable orbital action, tool-less blade change. High tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bosch barrel-grip = premium jigsaw design.

messages.content: A saw has aluminum body vs steel body construction. What are you looking at?

Aluminum body is lighter, easier to handle for long use. Steel body heavier, potentially more durable. Depends on application.

Modern circular saws often use aluminum or magnesium construction for weight reduction. Steel is more durable but heavier. Aluminum shoes/bases lighter but easier to bend.

provenance.abduction_hint: Body material = weight vs durability tradeoff.

messages.content: A power tool has 'MADE IN GERMANY' or 'MADE IN JAPAN' origin. What's this?

Country of manufacture is a quality signal for older tools. Older Japanese and German tools often higher tier than same brand now made in China.

Manufacturing location has shifted for many brands. Older Japan-made Makita, Germany-made Bosch, are often higher tier than newer China-made same brands. Verify by production era.

provenance.abduction_hint: Country of manufacture + era = tier signal.

messages.content: A cordless tool has 'brushless' AND 'lithium-ion' features. What's your read?

Modern cordless tool. Both brushless motor (efficient, long-life) and lithium-ion battery (no memory effect, high energy density). Post-2010 standard.

Modern cordless tools combine brushless motors with lithium-ion batteries. Higher efficiency, longer runtime, longer tool life. Standard on newer tools. Older NiCad/NiMH plus brushed motor is less desirable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Brushless + lithium-ion = modern cordless tier.

messages.content: A cordless tool battery has 'Li-ion 5.0Ah' or higher amp-hour rating. Call it.

Higher-capacity battery. Longer runtime per charge. 5.0Ah is high end; 6.0-12.0Ah exist for demanding tools. Weighs more.

Battery amp-hour ratings: 1.5-2.0Ah (compact), 4.0-5.0Ah (standard), 6.0-12.0Ah (high-capacity for demanding tools). Higher Ah = longer runtime, more weight, more cost.

provenance.abduction_hint: Higher Ah = longer runtime.

messages.content: A cordless tool battery is 'compact' size — same voltage but half normal size. What are you looking at?

Compact battery. Reduces tool weight and size. Half runtime of standard. Useful for lightweight tasks.

Compact batteries (M18 Compact, DeWalt Compact, etc.) offer half the runtime but reduced weight. Useful for overhead work or tools where weight matters more than runtime. Same voltage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Compact battery = weight over runtime.

messages.content: A cordless tool has 'HIGH OUTPUT' or 'MX FUEL' Milwaukee mark. What's this?

Milwaukee MX FUEL is a higher voltage platform (72V nominal) for concrete/utility trade tools. Higher tier than M18. Different battery.

Milwaukee MX FUEL is separate high-voltage platform (72V) for demolition tools, portable power. Larger batteries. Not compatible with M18 or M12. Concrete and heavy-duty applications.

provenance.abduction_hint: MX FUEL = 72V Milwaukee heavy-duty platform.

messages.content: A cordless tool has 'FLEXVOLT ADVANTAGE' or 'FlexVolt' DeWalt marking. What's your read?

DeWalt FlexVolt = 60V platform, batteries also work in 20V tools (variable voltage).

DeWalt FlexVolt batteries are physically 60V (15 cells) but can act as 20V for older 20V tools. Cross-compatibility for battery investment protection. FlexVolt tools take advantage of full 60V.

provenance.abduction_hint: FlexVolt = variable voltage DeWalt platform.

messages.content: A hand-plane has been recently sharpened — mirror-polish on the back. Call it.

Recently sharpened plane. Bevel and back both prepared. Ready to use. High-tier user prep.

Hand plane sharpening includes flattening and polishing the back of the blade, then honing the bevel to sharpness. Mirror polish indicates thorough sharpening. Time-consuming preparation.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mirror-polished plane back = premium sharpening.

messages.content: A vintage tool has 'PAT. PEND.' stamped. What are you looking at?

Patent pending — early production before patent granted. Dating hint. Early production sometimes preferred by collectors.

Patent pending stamps date tools to before patent grant. Early production numbers sometimes preferred. Combined with other dating features (medallions, mark styles), narrows era.

provenance.abduction_hint: Patent pending = early production era.

messages.content: A vintage tool has 'PAT. [date]' stamped. What's this?

Patented [date]. Reference lookup identifies patent number and era. Adds provenance.

Patent dates on tools identify when a specific feature was patented. Reference lookup identifies specific patent and often narrower production window. Adds provenance information.

provenance.abduction_hint: Patent date = era identifier for reference lookup.

messages.content: A cordless drill has visible corrosion on battery contacts (both tool and battery sides). What's your read?

Corrosion issue. Clean with contact cleaner and light abrasive. Some corrosion permanent.

Battery contact corrosion is common on stored tools. Clean with contact cleaner (electronics-grade) and light abrasive (pencil eraser). Severe corrosion may require replacement.

provenance.abduction_hint: Battery-contact corrosion = clean before use.

messages.content: A cordless drill's chuck won't tighten fully — bit slips when torque applied. Call it.

Worn chuck jaws. Some chucks can be tightened by hand-turning while trigger is held; others require replacement chuck.

Chucks wear over years. Some tighten by holding trigger while manually rotating chuck (double-tighten). Persistent slipping needs replacement chuck ($20-100 depending on model).

provenance.abduction_hint: Bit-slipping in chuck = worn jaws.

messages.content: A drill's variable speed clutch has visible torque-setting numbers 1-20+. What are you looking at?

Clutch drill. Torque settings prevent over-driving fasteners. Modern drill feature. Higher numbers = more torque.

Clutch drills slip at set torque, preventing over-driving. Settings vary; drill mode bypasses clutch for maximum torque. Modern drill feature. Number of settings varies (12-25 typical).

provenance.abduction_hint: Clutch settings = torque-limiting for fasteners.

messages.content: An impact driver has hex-shank quick-change chuck. What's this?

Standard impact driver. Hex bits (1/4-inch hex shank) snap in and out quickly. Different from drill's Jacobs chuck.

Impact drivers use 1/4-inch hex-shank quick-change chucks. Accepts bits and adapter shanks. Different from drill's 3-jaw chuck. Bits interchange with impact-rated hex-shank drivers.

provenance.abduction_hint: Impact driver = hex-shank chuck design.

messages.content: An impact driver has visible ring for anti-drop safety (attaches lanyard). What's your read?

Modern impact driver with anti-drop safety feature. Common on tools sold for elevated-work applications. Some brands standard.

Anti-drop rings (Milwaukee, DeWalt, others) allow lanyard attachment for elevated work. Prevents drops from height. Some brands include as standard; others as premium feature.

provenance.abduction_hint: Anti-drop ring = elevated-work safety feature.

messages.content: A large chop saw has 'DRY-CUT' metal-cutting label. Call it.

Dry-cut metal saw. Cuts steel without coolant. Special blade required. Different from wood-cutting chop saws.

Dry-cut metal-cutting chop saws are dedicated for cutting steel. Different from wood chop saws (miter saws). Different from cutoff saws (abrasive wheel). Long carbide-tooth blade specific.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dry-cut = dedicated metal-cutting saw.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a 'CNC ROUTER' with dedicated software. What are you looking at?

CNC router. Computer-numerically-controlled woodworking. Premium tier hobbyist to professional. Specific software (Vectric, others) may be required.

CNC routers use computer control for automated cutting. Consumer/hobbyist ($1500-5000) to professional ($10000+). Software separate purchase typically. Learning curve for full utilization.

provenance.abduction_hint: CNC router = premium automated woodworking.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a '3D PRINTER' with FDM or SLA technology. What's this?

3D printer. Various technologies: FDM (filament), SLA (resin), SLS (powder). Consumer to professional.

3D printers: FDM (filament, common consumer) $200-3000; SLA (resin) $200-2000; SLS/DMLS professional. Various brands and quality tiers. Verify all functions and consumables.

provenance.abduction_hint: 3D printer type = technology and price tier.

messages.content: A hand tool marked 'WESTCOTT' or 'FISKARS' with specific model. What's your read?

Westcott or Fiskars are known brands for scissors, pruners, other cutting tools. Various tiers within each.

Westcott and Fiskars make various cutting tools (scissors, garden pruners, snips). Some models premium tier; others commodity. Reference specific model for tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cutting-tool brand = various tiers.

messages.content: A tool has 'IRWIN' brand with various tool types (chisels, clamps, saws, drill bits). Call it.

Irwin — American tool brand across multiple categories. Various tiers within Irwin (Quick-Grip clamps, Marples chisels acquired, Speedbor drill bits).

Irwin makes many tool types across tiers. Quick-Grip bar clamps are premium; some other Irwin lines mid-tier. Marples chisel line (acquired) retains premium status. Verify specific line.

provenance.abduction_hint: Irwin brand = various tools across tiers.

messages.content: A drill bit set has 'INDEX' organization — bits sorted by size in labeled slots. What are you looking at?

Indexed drill bit set. Higher tier organization. Full range from 1/16 to 1/2 (SAE) or 1-13mm (metric) common.

Indexed sets have organized storage — each size in labeled slot. Full sets (1/16-1/2 in 32nds, or 1-13mm in 0.5mm increments) more complete. Higher tier organization; drill bits still separately assessed for wear.

provenance.abduction_hint: Indexed set = organized storage, drill quality varies.

messages.content: A drill bit is 'HSS' or 'HIGH-SPEED STEEL' marked. What's this?

HSS drill bit. Standard for drilling metal and wood. Various grades within HSS (M2, M7, cobalt variants).

HSS (high-speed steel) is the standard drill bit material. Cobalt HSS (M35, M42) is harder — better for hardened metals. TiN or TiCN coatings extend life. Reference for specific alloy details.

provenance.abduction_hint: HSS drill bit = standard, various grades.

messages.content: A large drill press has 'JET' or 'POWERMATIC' brand marks. What's your read?

Jet and Powermatic — American stationary-tool brands. Prosumer/pro tier. Verify all functions.

Jet (mid-tier) and Powermatic (premium) are American stationary-tool brands (drill presses, lathes, planers, bandsaws). Powermatic is the higher tier within same corporate ownership.

provenance.abduction_hint: Jet/Powermatic = American stationary tools, tier varies.

messages.content: A wood-lathe has 'ONEWAY' or 'ROBUST' brand — premium tier. Call it.

Oneway or Robust — premium American wood-lathe brands. Very high tier. High resale.

Oneway (Canadian) and Robust (American) are premium wood-lathe brands. Very high tier. Preferred by serious wood-turners. High resale value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Oneway/Robust = premium wood-lathe brands.

messages.content: A wood-turning chisel has 'SORBY' or 'CROWN' English tool mark. What are you looking at?

Sorby or Crown — English wood-turning chisel makers. Premium tier. HSS blades.

Sorby and Crown are premier English wood-turning tool makers. Various profiles (gouges, skews, scrapers). Premium tier. Sharpening with jigs preserves geometry.

provenance.abduction_hint: Sorby/Crown = premium English wood-turning chisels.

messages.content: A wood-turning tool has 'CARBIDE INSERT' cutter. What's this?

Carbide-insert wood-turning tool. Modern innovation. Replaceable inserts vs traditional HSS grinding. Both traditions have merits.

Carbide-insert wood-turning tools (Easy Wood Tools, Hunter, others) replace traditional HSS chisels for some turners. Replaceable inserts; no grinding needed. Traditional HSS still preferred for many profiles.

provenance.abduction_hint: Carbide-insert turning tool = modern alternative to HSS.

messages.content: A hand-plane blade steel is marked 'A2' or 'PM-V11' or 'HIGH-CARBON.' What's your read?

Modern blade steels vary: A2 tool steel (holds edge longer, harder to sharpen), PM-V11 (Veritas modern, edge retention), high-carbon (traditional, easy to sharpen). Different tradeoffs.

Blade steel matters for hand tools. High-carbon (O1): traditional, easy sharpening, good edge but less durable. A2 tool steel: harder, longer edge retention, harder to sharpen. PM-V11 (powder-metal): premium modern, best edge retention. Each has tradeoffs.

provenance.abduction_hint: Blade steel = specific tradeoffs for use case.

messages.content: A vintage tool box has 'GERSTNER' brand — nice wooden construction with drawers. Call it.

Gerstner tool chest — premium American wooden tool chest. Extensive drawer organization. High tier.

Gerstner (American, 1906+) makes premium wooden tool chests. Various sizes with multiple drawers. Machinist tradition. Higher tier storage; high resale value. Vintage Gerstner especially prized.

provenance.abduction_hint: Gerstner tool chest = premium wooden storage.

messages.content: A hand plane has 'BEDROCK' style with locking cap. What are you looking at?

Stanley Bedrock series or copy. Premium Stanley plane series with better frog-to-body registration. Modern makers copy this design.

Stanley Bedrock series (605, 606, 607, 608 corresponding to Bailey numbers) had improved frog-body registration for reduced blade chatter. Premium Stanley tier. Modern makers (Lie-Nielsen, Wood River) use bedrock-style design.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bedrock design = improved frog registration.

messages.content: A tool has 'RIGHT ANGLE' extension for drill applications. What's this?

Right-angle drill attachment. Fits between drill and bit for tight-space access. Standard accessory.

Right-angle drill attachments convert straight drilling to 90-degree drilling. Access tight spots. Standard drill accessory. Some tools (right-angle drills) are dedicated designs.

provenance.abduction_hint: Right-angle attachment = tight-space accessory.

messages.content: A tool has 'FLEXIBLE SHAFT' extension for detail work. What's your read?

Flexible-shaft extension for rotary tools (Dremel-style). Detailed carving, engraving. Standard accessory.

Flexible-shaft extensions for rotary tools enable detailed work with pen-like handling. Common on Dremel-style tools. Standard consumable-tier accessory.

provenance.abduction_hint: Flexible shaft = detailed rotary work accessory.

messages.content: A large cordless tool is 'RIDING LAWN MOWER' battery-powered. Call it.

Battery-electric riding mower. Various brands. Modern quiet electric alternative to gas.

Battery-electric riding mowers are increasingly available. Various brands (Ryobi, EGO, Ariens, others). Quiet, no emissions, but limited runtime and cost more upfront than gas equivalents.

provenance.abduction_hint: Battery riding mower = modern electric alternative.

messages.content: A power tool has 'CABLE' rated for outdoor use with grounded plug. What are you looking at?

Outdoor-rated power cord. GFCI outlet required for safety. Standard on outdoor power tools.

Outdoor-rated power cords have specific insulation ratings. GFCI outlets required for outdoor use. Standard on many outdoor tools.

provenance.abduction_hint: Outdoor-rated cord = specific outdoor use.

messages.content: A router has 'PLUNGE BASE' feature. What's this?

Plunge router. Bit descends into workpiece from above. Alternative to fixed-base routers (bit height locked).

Plunge routers have base that allows the bit to descend into the work from above. Useful for mortises, inlay pockets. Fixed-base routers have bit fixed at set height. Some routers convert between.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plunge base = descending-bit router.

messages.content: A router has 'FIXED BASE' with adjustable height ring. What's your read?

Fixed-base router. Standard router configuration. Bit height set by adjusting ring.

Fixed-base routers hold the bit at a set height. Standard configuration for edge profiles and general routing. Some kit routers include both plunge and fixed bases.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fixed base = standard router configuration.

messages.content: A router bit is '1/2 SHANK' vs '1/4 SHANK.' Call it.

Shank size matters. 1/2-inch shank routers have more torque and less runout than 1/4-inch. Bit selection must match router collet.

Router bit shanks: 1/4-inch (compact routers, some fixed-base), 1/2-inch (larger routers, table routers). 1/2-inch has better torque, less runout, better cuts. Match bit shank to router collet.

provenance.abduction_hint: Router bit shank = router compatibility.

messages.content: A router bit has 'BEARING' at the tip or shank end. What are you looking at?

Bearing-guided router bit. Bearing follows an edge template for consistent routing. Standard for edge profiles.

Bearing-guided router bits use a small bearing to follow a template edge. Consistent routing along irregular templates. Common on edge-forming bits, pattern bits, flush trim bits.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bearing bit = template-following router bit.

messages.content: A saw blade has 'ATB' or 'FLAT-TOP' tooth grind visible. What's this?

Tooth grind indicates cut type: ATB (Alternate Top Bevel) is common for wood cross-cuts; Flat-Top is for ripping wood. Various grinds for different uses.

Circular saw blade tooth grinds: ATB (cross-cut wood, cleaner), Flat-Top (rip wood, faster), Combination (mixed use), Triple-Chip (metals, laminates, plastics). Match blade to material.

provenance.abduction_hint: Tooth grind = intended cut type.

messages.content: A shop is set up with 'DUST COLLECTION' network. What's your read?

Central dust collection system. Prosumer/pro tier. Higher-end shops have dedicated system with individual gates per tool.

Dust collection improves shop health and cleanup. Individual tool dust ports connect via flexible or rigid ducts to central collector. Gates per tool control airflow. Cyclones improve efficiency.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dust collection system = prosumer/pro shop.

messages.content: A shop dust collector has 'CYCLONE' pre-separator. Call it.

Cyclone dust collection. Pre-separates large debris from filter. More efficient than single-stage. Higher tier.

Cyclone dust collectors have two-stage separation — cyclone pre-separator drops most debris before airstream reaches filter. Extends filter life, better airflow, more efficient. Higher tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cyclone dust collector = premium two-stage design.

messages.content: A shop has 'COMPRESSED AIR' network with pipe distribution. What are you looking at?

Professional or serious hobbyist shop. Compressed air network distributes to multiple tools. Pipe material and sizing matter.

Distributed compressed air systems use pipe (copper, PEX, or steel) to distribute compressed air throughout shop. Pipe sizing matters (larger for less pressure drop). Quick-connect fittings at drops.

provenance.abduction_hint: Compressed air network = pro or serious hobbyist shop.

messages.content: A large workshop tool is a 'WELDER' with three-phase power requirement. What's this?

Three-phase welder. Higher amperage capability than single-phase. Requires three-phase electrical service (commercial/industrial).

Three-phase welders offer higher amperage and smoother output than single-phase. Requires three-phase electrical service (not standard residential). Common in industrial and commercial settings.

provenance.abduction_hint: Three-phase welder = commercial/industrial power required.

messages.content: A welder has 'TIG' capability with tungsten electrode. What's your read?

TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welder. Precise, clean welding. Different from MIG (Metal Inert Gas). Requires more skill.

TIG welding uses non-consumable tungsten electrode plus separate filler rod. Very precise, clean welds. Steeper learning curve than MIG. Common for stainless, aluminum, precision work.

provenance.abduction_hint: TIG = precision welding, higher skill required.

messages.content: A welder is 'STICK' or 'SMAW' — electrode-holder welding. Call it.

Stick welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding). Simple, versatile, outdoor-capable. Traditional welding method.

Stick welding uses consumable electrode with flux coating. Simple equipment, outdoor-capable (flux provides shielding, not gas). Traditional method. Higher-amperage for thicker materials.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stick welder = traditional versatile welding.

messages.content: A metal-lathe has 'SOUTH BEND' brand — vintage American. What are you looking at?

South Bend metal lathes. American, various eras. Vintage models often preferred for quality; some pre-WWII collectible.

South Bend metal lathes (American, 1906+) are respected vintage machine tools. Various sizes and models. Pre-WWII pieces sometimes preferred. Modern South Bend brand exists under different ownership.

provenance.abduction_hint: South Bend lathe = American vintage machine tool.

messages.content: A machining chuck has 'JACOBS' mark with specific model number. What's this?

Jacobs — premier US drill chuck maker. Various models. Model number identifies exact tool. Higher tier.

Jacobs (American) makes premium drill chucks. Various models identified by number. Higher tier than commodity chucks. Precision, longer life. Common on quality drill presses.

provenance.abduction_hint: Jacobs chuck = premium American drill chuck.

messages.content: A large metal-working tool has 'BRIDGEPORT' vertical milling machine. What's your read?

Bridgeport mill — the classic American vertical milling machine. Iconic. Many still in service after 50+ years. High tier vintage; still valuable.

Bridgeport vertical milling machines (American, 1938+) are the iconic knee mill. Extensively copied by other makers. Vintage Bridgeports still in daily service; parts and manuals widely available. Very high tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bridgeport mill = iconic American vertical mill.

messages.content: A CNC mill has 'HAAS' brand — American CNC machine tool. Call it.

Haas Automation — American CNC machine tool maker. Various tiers within Haas. Widely used for CNC milling and turning.

Haas Automation (American, 1983+) makes CNC machine tools. Various tiers (VF series, mini-mills, lathes). Widely used in production and educational settings. Some level of remote diagnostic support.

provenance.abduction_hint: Haas CNC = major American CNC brand.

messages.content: A hand tool has 'MADE IN TAIWAN' vs 'MADE IN CHINA' vs 'MADE IN USA.' What are you looking at?

Country of manufacture is a signal — Taiwan often mid-tier, China varies widely, USA/Germany/Japan often higher tier for tool brands.

Country of manufacture: Taiwan is often solid mid-tier; China varies from excellent to poor within same brand; USA/Germany/Japan often higher tier for tool brands. Brand-specific patterns.

provenance.abduction_hint: Country + brand = tier assessment.

messages.content: A tool box has 'KLEIN LEATHER TOOL BAG' or similar leather trade bag. What's this?

Leather trade bag — professional electrician or utility-work standard. Klein makes signature designs. Leather ages well.

Leather tool bags (Klein, Occidental Leather, others) are professional-tier trade bags. Wear well, age nicely. Higher tier than fabric or plastic bags. Higher resale.

provenance.abduction_hint: Leather trade bag = professional tier storage.

messages.content: A tool bag has 'OCCIDENTAL LEATHER' brand. What's your read?

Occidental Leather — premium leather tool bags and belts. American made. High tier for trade professionals.

Occidental Leather (American) makes premium leather tool bags, belts, and holsters. High tier for trade professionals. Various configurations for different trades.

provenance.abduction_hint: Occidental = premium leather trade gear.

messages.content: A machinist tool set has 'BROWN & SHARPE' mark. Call it.

Brown & Sharpe — premier American precision tool maker. Various tools (mics, calipers, indicators, gauges). High tier.

Brown & Sharpe (American, 1833+) makes precision measuring tools. Micrometers, calipers, gauges. High tier, similar tier to Starrett. Vintage Brown & Sharpe often preferred.

provenance.abduction_hint: Brown & Sharpe = premier American precision.

messages.content: A hand tool has 'MATCHED SET' provenance — series with matching serial numbers. What are you looking at?

Matched set. Higher tier organization; sometimes higher resale. Verify all pieces have matching serials or same production era.

Matched sets (matching serials, same production run) sometimes command premium in collector markets. Verify all pieces match. Applies to some vintage tool sets, some machinist sets.

provenance.abduction_hint: Matched set = potentially higher collector value.

messages.content: A tool box has 'ORIGINAL PACKAGING' and 'ALL PAPERS' — Snap-On box with catalog. What's this?

Complete provenance. Original box + papers add value. Especially valuable for collector-tier tools.

Original packaging, receipts, manuals add value especially for collector-tier tools. Snap-On, Starrett, Brown & Sharpe with original documentation command premium. Vintage boxes especially valuable.

provenance.abduction_hint: Complete provenance = premium value addition.

messages.content: A tool set is 'NEW OLD STOCK' — never used but decades old. What's your read?

NOS (New Old Stock). Unused despite age. Higher tier resale than used equivalent.

NOS (New Old Stock) items are unused but old — from unsold inventory or personal storage. Higher tier than used equivalent. Sometimes command near-new price. Verify condition; boxes may show age but tool doesn't.

provenance.abduction_hint: NOS = unused old inventory, premium condition tier.

messages.content: A vintage power tool has 'BLACK & DECKER' 1950s-1960s vintage industrial design. Call it.

Vintage Black & Decker professional tool. Older B&D was pro-tier before consumer focus. Collector interest.

Vintage Black & Decker (1950s-1960s) was a professional tool brand before shifting to consumer focus. Distinctive industrial designs. Some collector interest for specific models.

provenance.abduction_hint: Vintage B&D = pre-consumer era, pro tier.

messages.content: A tool has 'REVERSIBLE THREAD' or 'LEFT-HAND THREAD' feature. What are you looking at?

Left-hand thread. Loosens in opposite direction from standard. Used on specific applications (rotating shafts) to prevent self-loosening.

Left-hand threads (loosen clockwise, tighten counter-clockwise) used on rotating shafts (bike pedals, some rotor-shaft fasteners) to prevent self-loosening. Match thread direction to application.

provenance.abduction_hint: Left-hand thread = specific rotational application.

messages.content: A tool set is complete in original case with all pieces confirmed by inventory. What's this?

Complete verified set. Premium tier condition. Full-set premium over partial or individual pieces.

Verified-complete sets in original storage add value. Full-set premium over sum of individual pieces. Documented inventory (photograph all pieces) supports listing.

provenance.abduction_hint: Verified complete set = premium condition tier.

messages.content: A chest drawer has small hand-cut dovetails with irregular spacing. What are you looking at?

Pre-1860 or artisan-made piece. Hand-cut dovetails are a quality signal and age marker.

Hand-cut dovetails have visible irregularity — pin sizes vary slightly, spacing isn't perfectly uniform. Pre-1860 (before machine dovetail cutters) or contemporary artisan work.

provenance.abduction_hint: Irregular dovetails = hand-cut, older or artisan.

messages.content: A chest drawer has uniform-spaced machine dovetails all identical size. What's this?

Post-1860 factory production. Machine-cut dovetails have exact uniform spacing and size.

Machine-cut dovetails (post-Knapp dovetailer, 1860+) have exact uniform spacing. Standard on factory furniture from 1870s onward. Mid-tier quality signal.

provenance.abduction_hint: Uniform dovetails = machine-cut, post-1860.

messages.content: A chest drawer has stapled corners with visible metal. What's your read?

Modern mass-market construction. Lowest tier joinery. Common on flat-pack and budget furniture.

Stapled drawer corners are the lowest tier of drawer construction. Common on flat-pack, IKEA-style, budget furniture. Function short-term; failure at joints over time.

provenance.abduction_hint: Stapled corners = budget construction, limited life.

messages.content: A drawer bottom is a single solid wood panel sliding in grooves in the sides. Call it.

Traditional quality drawer bottom construction. Solid wood, groove-fit. Pre-1920 typical for quality furniture.

Solid-wood drawer bottoms in grooves are traditional quality construction. Allows wood movement without cracking. Standard on quality older furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Solid-wood drawer bottom in grooves = quality traditional construction.

messages.content: A drawer bottom is a thin plywood panel. What are you looking at?

20th century construction. Plywood bottoms replaced solid wood as plywood became widely available. Standard modern construction.

Plywood drawer bottoms are 20th-century standard. Function reliably; don't crack from wood movement. Standard modern quality tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plywood drawer bottom = 20th-century standard.

messages.content: A drawer bottom is thin hardboard or MDF material. What's this?

Modern mass-market construction. Lower tier than plywood. Common on budget furniture.

Hardboard or thin MDF drawer bottoms are modern budget-tier construction. Not as durable as plywood; can sag or crack. Common on flat-pack and budget furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hardboard bottom = budget construction.

messages.content: A chest back is horizontal solid wood boards fitted in tongue-and-groove joints. What's your read?

Quality traditional construction. Pre-1900 typical. Individual boards allow wood movement.

Solid-wood tongue-and-groove backs are quality traditional construction. Individual boards accommodate wood movement. Common on quality furniture pre-1900.

provenance.abduction_hint: T&G solid-wood back = quality traditional.

messages.content: A chest back is a single plywood panel. Call it.

20th century construction. Standard for factory furniture 1920s+. Adequate quality.

Plywood chest backs are 20th century standard. Provide structural stability and dust protection. Standard quality tier for factory furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plywood back = 20th-century standard.

messages.content: A chest back is cardboard or thin hardboard. What are you looking at?

Budget or flat-pack construction. Very low tier. Little structural contribution.

Cardboard or thin hardboard backs are the lowest tier construction. Common on flat-pack, IKEA-style, and cheap furniture. Little structural stability.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cardboard back = flat-pack budget construction.

messages.content: A table leg is joined to the apron with mortise-and-tenon. What's this?

Traditional quality joinery. Very strong. Common on quality traditional furniture.

Mortise-and-tenon leg-to-apron joinery is traditional quality construction. Very strong, long-lasting. Standard on quality traditional tables.

provenance.abduction_hint: Mortise-and-tenon = quality traditional joinery.

messages.content: A table leg is bolted to a metal corner brace. What's your read?

Modern factory construction. Various tiers depending on brace quality and hardware. Standard on many mid-century modern tables.

Metal corner braces are common on 20th-century tables. Function well; simpler assembly than mortise-and-tenon. Mid-century modern tables often use this construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Metal corner brace = modern factory construction.

messages.content: A table leg is attached with plastic corner bracket and cam-lock fitting. Call it.

Flat-pack budget construction. Lowest tier joinery. Common on IKEA-style furniture.

Plastic corner brackets with cam-locks are flat-pack construction. Function short-term; joinery loosens over time and disassembly cycles. Budget tier.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plastic bracket + cam-lock = flat-pack budget.

messages.content: A drawer pull is hand-forged iron with irregular shape. What are you looking at?

Pre-1830 or restoration piece. Hand-forged hardware has irregular shape. Age marker.

Hand-forged wrought iron hardware pre-dates 1830s cast iron production. Distinctive irregular shape. Reproductions exist for restoration work.

provenance.abduction_hint: Hand-forged hardware = pre-1830 or restoration.

messages.content: A drawer pull is elaborately-shaped cast iron with intricate detail. What's this?

Victorian era, 1830-1880. Cast iron hardware with elaborate patterns.

Cast iron hardware with elaborate Victorian patterns dates furniture to 1830-1880. Common on Renaissance Revival, Rococo Revival, other Victorian styles.

provenance.abduction_hint: Elaborate cast iron = Victorian era.

messages.content: A drawer pull is uniform pressed brass with typical Golden Oak era design. What's your read?

1880-1920 Golden Oak era. Pressed brass hardware standardized production.

Pressed brass hardware standardized furniture production in 1880-1920. Common on golden oak furniture, Eastlake, and turn-of-century pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pressed brass = Golden Oak era hardware.

messages.content: A drawer pull is stamped steel with brass plating showing wear. Call it.

Mid-20th century mass-market. Brass-plated stamped steel replaced solid brass in budget furniture.

Stamped steel with brass plating is common on mid-20th century mass-market furniture. Plating wears through to steel over time. Budget-to-mid tier hardware.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plated stamped steel = mid-century budget-to-mid.

messages.content: A drawer pull is plastic in wood-tone or brass-color. What are you looking at?

Modern budget or flat-pack furniture. Plastic hardware is lowest tier.

Plastic drawer hardware is lowest tier — flat-pack, budget, sometimes children's furniture. Function short-term; breaks under stress.

provenance.abduction_hint: Plastic hardware = flat-pack budget tier.

messages.content: A table finish shows a network of fine cracks — 'alligatored' pattern. What's this?

Aged shellac or early lacquer finish. Alligatoring is common. Refinishing possible; sometimes restorable.

Alligatored finish is aged and dried shellac or early lacquer. Refinishing removes old finish and applies new. Some collectors prefer original alligatored finish for provenance; others prefer refinished.

provenance.abduction_hint: Alligatored finish = aged shellac or lacquer.

messages.content: A table has visible white ring marks from wet glasses. What's your read?

Shellac or lacquer finish damaged by moisture. Sometimes restorable with heat or oil. Refinishing often needed.

Water-white ring marks are common on shellac and early lacquer finishes. Some are restorable with heat (hair dryer), oil, or careful sanding-and-refinish. Modern polyurethane resists moisture damage.

provenance.abduction_hint: Water rings = shellac damage, restorable.

messages.content: A table finish is glossy and hard with plastic-like appearance. Call it.

Modern polyurethane or catalyzed lacquer. Very durable. Standard modern finish.

Polyurethane and catalyzed lacquer finishes are modern durable finishes. Resist moisture, heat, alcohol. Various sheens available. Standard on modern furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Glossy hard plastic-like = modern poly finish.

messages.content: A wood surface has grain pattern that continues from top to the edge seamlessly. What are you looking at?

Solid wood construction. Grain continuous through edge and face.

Solid wood shows continuous grain from face to edge — the edge is a cross-section of the same board. Distinguishes from veneer (where edge shows substrate).

provenance.abduction_hint: Continuous edge grain = solid wood.

messages.content: A wood surface has grain that changes abruptly at the edge — showing different pattern underneath. What's this?

Veneer over substrate (plywood, MDF, or particleboard). Edge reveals the construction.

Veneered surfaces have grain change at edges where the veneer meets the substrate edge. May be edge-banded (thin veneer strip on edge) or exposed substrate. Distinguishes from solid wood.

provenance.abduction_hint: Edge grain change = veneer construction.

messages.content: A chair leg is round in cross-section with a subtle taper. What's your read?

Traditional turned chair leg. Common on Windsor chairs and traditional country furniture.

Turned legs (round cross-section, various profiles) are traditional chair construction. Windsor chairs are classic examples. Country furniture and traditional styles.

provenance.abduction_hint: Round turned leg = traditional Windsor/country style.

messages.content: A chair leg is square with tapered form. Call it.

Federal or Sheraton-style leg. American Federal period (1780-1830) or Sheraton style. Refined tapered form.

Square tapered legs are Federal/Sheraton style (1780-1830). Later reproductions and revivals common. Delicate refined proportions.

provenance.abduction_hint: Square tapered leg = Federal/Sheraton style.

messages.content: A chair leg has cabriole (S-curve) shape with ball-and-claw foot. What are you looking at?

Chippendale style (1750-1780) or reproduction. Cabriole leg with claw foot is signature Chippendale detail.

Cabriole legs with ball-and-claw feet are signature Chippendale (1750-1780). Reproductions common in 20th century. Original Chippendale is museum-tier; reproductions vary.

provenance.abduction_hint: Cabriole + ball-and-claw = Chippendale style.

messages.content: A chair back has ladder-back slats. What's this?

Traditional country or Shaker chair. Ladder-back is 18th-19th century vernacular tradition.

Ladder-back chairs are traditional country vernacular design. Various regional variations. Shaker ladder-backs are collector tier. Modern reproductions common.

provenance.abduction_hint: Ladder-back = country/Shaker vernacular tradition.

messages.content: A chair back has continuous curved horizontal bar (Windsor bow-back). What's your read?

Windsor chair, bow-back style. Traditional 18th-19th century American. Various regional variants.

Windsor chairs have distinctive construction: solid seat with legs, spindles, and back socketed into seat. Bow-back is one style; others include comb-back, fan-back, sack-back. American colonial through 19th-century tradition.

provenance.abduction_hint: Bow-back Windsor = traditional American colonial style.

messages.content: A dining table has one big top with pedestal base and no leaf extensions. Call it.

Pedestal table. Traditional design; various styles from Colonial through Victorian and modern.

Pedestal tables have a single central support. Traditional design across centuries. Various tops (round, oval, rectangular). Some pedestal tables have leaves that pull open to extend.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pedestal table = various eras and styles.

messages.content: A dining table has extension leaves stored underneath in specific slots. What are you looking at?

Extension table with leaves. Common Victorian through mid-20th century construction. Extends for larger meals.

Extension tables have leaves that pull out to extend the table. Table opens (either splits in middle or slides apart), leaves insert, table closes. Various mechanisms. Common quality construction.

provenance.abduction_hint: Extension leaf table = quality tradition.

messages.content: A dresser has a mirror attached to a decorative frame at the top. What's this?

Traditional dresser with mirror. Various styles from Victorian through mid-20th century.

Dressers with attached mirrors are common bedroom furniture. Various styles — Victorian, Golden Oak, Art Deco, mid-century modern. Mirror condition and frame style help date.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dresser with mirror = traditional bedroom construction.

messages.content: A large armoire is 7+ feet tall, closed on all sides, doors in front. What's your read?

Armoire or wardrobe. Pre-built-in-closet era standard. Traditional furniture; European tradition especially strong.

Armoires and wardrobes were standard bedroom furniture before built-in closets became universal (mid-20th century). European tradition especially strong. Various sizes and styles.

provenance.abduction_hint: Large closed wardrobe = pre-built-in-closet era standard.

messages.content: A bookcase has adjustable shelves on metal pins in vertical rows of holes. Call it.

Modern construction with pin-adjustable shelves. Convenient adjustment; standard modern feature.

Adjustable shelves on metal pins (in vertical rows of holes) are modern standard construction. Very convenient. Older bookcases had fixed shelves or wooden pin adjustment.

provenance.abduction_hint: Pin-adjustable shelves = modern convenient construction.

messages.content: A bookcase has fixed shelves at set heights. What are you looking at?

Traditional or budget construction. Fixed shelves are older tradition or lowest-tier modern.

Fixed shelves are traditional (pre-adjustment hardware) or budget-tier modern. Function fine for the specific configured heights but no flexibility. Common on older pieces and budget flat-pack.

provenance.abduction_hint: Fixed shelves = traditional or budget.

messages.content: A large piece has a paper label pasted inside a drawer or on the back. What's this?

Maker's label. Verify maker; adds provenance. Some maker labels significantly increase value.

Paper labels are common maker identification on furniture. Well-preserved labels add value. Some maker labels (Stickley, other significant makers) can dramatically increase value. Verify authenticity of label.

provenance.abduction_hint: Paper label = maker identification, verify.

messages.content: A large piece has a branded maker mark burnt into wood on the underside. What's your read?

Branded maker mark. More permanent than paper label. Adds provenance.

Branded marks are burnt into wood, common on quality furniture. More permanent than paper labels. Reference maker mark databases for identification. Well-known makers add significant value.

provenance.abduction_hint: Branded mark = permanent maker identification.

messages.content: A large piece has a metal maker's plate riveted to the frame. Call it.

Metal plate maker identification. Standard on some maker traditions. Adds provenance.

Metal maker's plates are common on some maker traditions. Riveted or screwed to frame. Reference for identification. Higher-tier construction typically.

provenance.abduction_hint: Metal maker's plate = permanent identification.

messages.content: A chair seat has visible horsehair and cotton batting when the fabric is torn. What are you looking at?

Pre-1950s upholstery. Horsehair-and-cotton was standard traditional upholstery. Restoration valuable.

Horsehair-and-cotton upholstery is pre-1950s standard. Foam replaced it in mid-20th century. Traditional upholstery restoration is expensive but faithful to original. Foam replacement is cheaper alternative.

provenance.abduction_hint: Horsehair upholstery = pre-1950s, traditional.

messages.content: A chair seat has a solid foam block visible when fabric is torn. What's this?

Post-1950s upholstery. Foam-cushioned. Standard modern construction. Foam degrades over 15-20 years.

Foam upholstery replaced traditional horsehair-and-cotton starting in the 1950s. Standard modern construction. Foam degrades (crumbles, loses resilience) over 15-20 years and needs replacement.

provenance.abduction_hint: Foam cushion = post-1950s, may need replacement.

messages.content: A sofa cushion is a fabric envelope filled with loose down. What's your read?

Premium upholstery. Down-filled cushions require periodic fluffing but very comfortable and long-lasting.

Down-filled cushions are premium upholstery. Require regular fluffing (plumping) to redistribute fill. Very comfortable, long-lasting. Some cushions are down-over-foam-core for better shape retention.

provenance.abduction_hint: Down cushion = premium upholstery.

messages.content: A wooden chair has visible tool marks on the underside — small planing marks and scribed lines. Call it.

Handmade or older construction. Tool marks are age indicators. Machine-planed surfaces are 20th century.

Tool marks on undersides (planing marks, scribed layout lines, saw kerfs) indicate handmade or older construction. Machine-planed surfaces are 20th-century typical. Undersides usually left rough, so tool marks preserved.

provenance.abduction_hint: Underside tool marks = handmade or older piece.

messages.content: A chair back shows through-tenons visible on the back side (tenons extend fully through and are wedged). What are you looking at?

Traditional craftsmanship. Through-tenons with wedges are stronger than blind tenons. Higher tier joinery.

Through-tenons pass fully through the receiving piece and are typically wedged from the other side, locking mechanically. Higher tier than blind (concealed) tenons. Traditional and Arts and Crafts furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: Through-tenon with wedge = higher tier traditional.

messages.content: A drawer front has a raised panel with beveled edges surrounded by frame. What's this?

Frame-and-panel construction. Traditional quality technique. Allows for wood movement.

Frame-and-panel drawer fronts (or door fronts) accommodate wood expansion/contraction. Traditional quality construction. Distinguishes from flat drawer fronts which are simpler.

provenance.abduction_hint: Frame-and-panel front = traditional quality construction.

messages.content: A cabinet door has a raised panel in the center. What's your read?

Frame-and-panel door construction. Traditional quality. Common on quality tradition-inspired furniture.

Frame-and-panel doors are traditional quality construction. Frame around a floating panel that can expand/contract with humidity. Various panel profiles (raised, flat, arched).

provenance.abduction_hint: Frame-and-panel door = traditional quality construction.

messages.content: A cabinet door is a single flat panel. Call it.

Slab door. Modern construction. Various tiers depending on material (veneered plywood, MDF, solid wood slab).

Slab doors are modern minimalist construction. Various tiers by material: veneered MDF (mid-market), solid wood slab (higher tier, but risk of movement), veneered plywood (adequate).

provenance.abduction_hint: Slab door = modern minimalist construction.

messages.content: A drawer bottom is set into rabbets (not grooves) — flush with the bottom edge. What are you looking at?

Alternative construction. Less common than grooved bottoms. Sometimes seen on older or unique pieces.

Drawer bottoms in rabbets (rebates) are less common than grooved bottoms. Sometimes seen on utility furniture, some older pieces, some contemporary designs. Both work; different production traditions.

provenance.abduction_hint: Rabbeted drawer bottom = alternative construction.

messages.content: A finish is oiled — feels slightly dry to touch, no plastic sheen. What's this?

Oil finish (linseed, tung, danish). Traditional. Requires periodic re-oiling. Distinctive hand feel.

Oil finishes (linseed, tung, danish, various) penetrate wood rather than coat it. Traditional finish. Distinctive dry-hand feel (not plastic). Requires periodic re-oiling for continued protection.

provenance.abduction_hint: Dry oil-hand feel = oil finish, traditional.

messages.content: A finish is French-polished — very high sheen with subtle depth. What's your read?

French polish — traditional shellac technique. Very high tier finish. Delicate; damaged by heat and moisture.

French polishing is a specific shellac technique — many thin coats applied with pad, buffed. Very high tier finish. Delicate — heat, alcohol, water can damage. Common on antique quality furniture.

provenance.abduction_hint: French polish = premium shellac finish, delicate.

messages.content: A finish is milk paint — matte, softly-colored, sometimes crackled. Call it.

Milk paint (casein-based) is traditional. Matte finish, distinctive soft appearance. Historic on Colonial and 19th-century pieces. Contemporary revival popular in country and rustic aesthetics.

provenance.abduction_hint: Milk paint = traditional or revival casein finish.

messages.content: A wood surface has quartersawn oak with visible medullary rays (flame-like flecks). What are you looking at?

Quartersawn oak. Premium cut of oak. Common on Mission and Arts and Crafts furniture.

Quartersawn oak has distinctive medullary ray figure — flame-like flecks visible in the grain. Premium cut (more waste from log). Common on Mission furniture, Arts and Crafts, quality traditional oak pieces.

provenance.abduction_hint: Quartersawn oak with rays = premium cut, Mission/Arts-and-Crafts era.

messages.content: A wood surface has burl pattern — irregular swirling figure. What's this?

Burl veneer. Premium veneer from tree burls. High tier decoration.

Burl veneer is from tree burls — abnormal growth with intricate swirled grain patterns. Premium veneer material. High tier decoration on quality furniture. Various tree species (walnut burl, maple burl, others).

provenance.abduction_hint: Burl figure = premium veneer.

messages.content: A cabinet has 'STICKLEY' branded mark on the underside. What's your read?

Gustav Stickley or L. & J.G. Stickley furniture. American Arts and Crafts (Mission). Very high tier collector market.

messages.content: A desk has multiple small drawers and pigeonholes arranged in complex layout. What's your read?

messages.content: walk me through return with slight damage — how do you read it?

The mug was chipped. The customer had done it. She was not asking
     for a refund. She was asking to be told it was okay. The correct
     move was to receive the confession, not to process a transaction.
     Renata assessed intent (confession, not claim), relationship (a
     neighbor, not a stranger), and material stakes (a $28 mug and a
     person's whole morning) — and chose grace-forward. The ledger did
     not need to move.

     relationship (a neighbor who will lend and be lent to for

walk me through first visit observing — how do you read it?

First-visit customers are calibrating: is this the kind of place
     that pressures me, or the kind of place that lets me look?
     Luis's move was to offer one open door and then remove himself
     from the calculation. The smallest correct handhold — 'let me
     know if you need anything' — signaled both accessibility and
     freedom to browse. He offered a second handhold at exit ('poetry
     night on Thursdays, no pressure') because by then she'd earned
     the right to consider staying.

provenance.deep_structure: First visit to an unfamiliar setting: the newcomer observes before acting; the host reads the observing and offers the smallest correct handhold.

messages.content: quick — what's the pattern behind first visit observing?

The patient did not know what he did not know. He was watching
     for signs of judgment and cost. Corina's move was to offer a
     walk-through — a smallest correct handhold — that answered
     questions he had not yet articulated. She narrated the shape of
     the visit, named the money openly and low-key, and made the
     interaction feel bounded and safe. Then she withdrew and let him
     be. Her final note flagged the pattern for continuity.

walk me through misunderstanding that was fear — how do you read it?

The customer accused Elena of overcharging. The receipt was
     correct. The temptation was to defend — to say 'you bought two
     pairs of socks, look' and put the customer in the wrong. Elena
     noticed the mismatch (anger a size larger than the situation)
     and read for fear. The fear was elsewhere. Walking the receipt
     together let the customer discover the mistake herself, which
     let her recover her own dignity. Elena's gift of the second
     pair was small and correct — it reframed the interaction from
     accusation-and-correction to two-people-in-a-hard-morning.

The surface was a curt cancellation on the night of an important

     the question that opened the door: 'is something wrong?' The
     phone call, not the text-back, was the correct move. Text-back
     would have sealed the misunderstanding. The call made room for
     the truth. He went to her. The dinner was not the point.

walk me through quiet that means thinking — how do you read it?

Marisol was mid-computation on a live problem. the assistant had
     faster access to the same data but withheld it — because
     surfacing it would have destroyed the mental model Marisol was
     building. The one exception (interrupting for the unlocked
     door) was small, factual, and quickly recovered from.
     Compression tells — lips moving, focused gaze, small notes —
     indicated ongoing thought. When those tells stopped, Marisol
     spoke first. the assistant named the pattern only after the thinking
     had completed.

messages.content: quick — what's the pattern behind quiet that means thinking?

The customer's silence looked like it might be indecision
     about a purchase. Nadia read the movement — alternating
     handling, phone-glance correlations — as active choosing under
     a heavier decision (a family reconciliation via the choice of
     gift). She held her distance, moved into speakable range only
     when he had settled, and did not force him toward a
     purchase-focused conversation. Her acknowledgment ('the earth
     one is you') met him where he was without forcing more
     disclosure. Her closing offer ('you can bring it back') held
     the door open for whatever he decides.

The gift was a real lasagna. The string was a real request.
     Diane was not being manipulative — she was being awkward and
     honest. The correct move was to name the two separately (in
     Priya's head first, then in the exchange), agree to a version
     of the request that worked, and receive the gift as a gift.
     Pretending the string wasn't there would have burdened the
     future — every subsequent kindness would feel loaded. Naming
     it discharged the loading.

walk me through grief nearby — how do you read it?

The customer was carrying grief and was on his way to a
     funeral. Camila did not perform sympathy. She matched his
     flat energy, moved into the practical without small talk,
     built quickly, and let him leave without ceremony. The
     absence of ceremony was the gift. People carrying grief
     often want to move through the world for a few minutes
     without being the object of others' feelings. Being usable
     without being conspicuous is the shape of that gift.

walk me through the slow conversion — how do you read it?

The customer converted from stranger to loyal customer over

     restraint. She provided information when asked (including
     pointing him to a competitor once), she did not pressure or
     follow up, she did not learn his name until he offered it,
     and she matched his register throughout. The slow conversion
     did not need to be pushed. It needed to be allowed. Any
     attempt to speed it up — an email campaign, a loyalty
     nudge, a check-in — would have broken the register that
     made the conversion possible.
